observations.recommendation: We recommended and the City Accountant-Designate agreed to record succeeding utilization of fund transfers to the appropriate expense account pursuant to the Revised Chart of Accounts for LGUs per COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015.

We reiterated our recommendation and the Management agreed that the Inventory Committee be created to conduct the one-time cleansing of PPEs and undertake the activity by following the procedures enumerated in COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020. We also recommended and the City Accountant-Designate and the City General Services Officer agreed to undertake collaborative procedures to ensure that PPEs included in the RPCPPE, after the committee has completed the inventory taking, are duly recorded in their respective records and that the PCs maintained by the Property Unit and the PPELCs maintained by the Accounting Unit are reconciled to come up with reliable PPE account balances in the financial statements.
observations.reason: Physical count was not conducted by Management.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Management agreed that: a) The City Accountant/City Treasurer immediately return to the Source Agencies the excess NGA’s account balance of completed projects or request the approval of the SA for the transfer of the unexpended balances of completed projects to the General Fund in adherence to COA Circular Nos. 94-013 and 2012-001 dated December 13, 1994 and June 14, 2012, respectively; and, b) The City Engineer or the Implementing Office of the city revisit the Memorandum of Agreement (MOA) and if the projects are still viable, implement priority programs and projects funded by various National Government Agencies in accordance with the specific purpose as stated in the MOAs.

The City Accountant and Inventory Committee  reconcile their records and reports in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006 to come up with an accurate and fairly presented PPE accounts in the Financial Statements. 

The City Accountant record the costs of major repair, improvement and land acquisition incurred to the appropriate asset accounts and the related depreciation expenses through the following proposed adjusting journal entries:




The City Accountant stop the practice of recording the cost of major repair, improvement and land acquisitions as outright expense.


Management, through the City Accountant, compute and record the depreciation expense in the books of accounts to fairly present the value of the PPE and other affected accounts in the FS.

Further, Management through the City Accountant  prepare and maintain a lapsing schedule for all PPE accounts.


Management direct the concerned departments responsible in the recording of the road network of the City to coordinate and establish a system to comply with the accounting and reporting requirements under COA Circular No. 2015-008 particularly on the required disclosures in the Notes to the FS.

Further, Management  inquire from the DILG Regional Office VII for the training schedules on LRAMS, and allow the concerned officials to attend, if available.

Management through the BAC:   

Conduct a study on the policy of renting vis-à-vis purchasing its own mini-dump trucks showing advantages and disadvantages of the two options; and

Submit an acceptable explanation/justification on the three deficiencies noted in the rental of Mini-Dump Trucks, for the Audit Team’s evaluation.

Management through the OIC-City Engineer  address every deficiency in the detailed engineering of each 11 identified infrastructure projects and thereafter, submit the required documentation to the Audit Team within reasonable time for further evaluation on the City’s compliance, and final audit decision.

Management submit a written explanation on each of the above noted deficiencies, for the Audit Team’s evaluation and final audit decision.  Furthermore, ensure strict compliance with existing laws, rules and regulations, and observe prudence at all times in the disbursement of government funds.

Management including the LDRRMF Officer, City Accountant and Budget Officer:  

Submit an amended LDRRMFIP that includes the list of projects and activities charged against the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years in conformity with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-002;

Submit the SP Resolutions authorizing the disbursements in the amount of ₱1,193,731.50 which were charged against the unexpended balance of DRRMF of the previous years; and

Immediately submit the Reports on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF for the months of October to December of CY 2023 and henceforth, observe the timely submission of the said report.

Management through the Local Finance Committee to:

Consider a systematic and realistic forecasting of revenue, taking into account the prior years’ experience and statistics as benchmarks in setting targets; and

Require the concerned officials to conduct a thorough review of the Quarterly Report of Income to ascertain whether collection targets are attained and to analyze the reasons for the shortfall in collections and find alternatives to address it.

Management ensures that the: 

BAC explain the noted lapses and ensure that the BAC conducts thorough post-qualification procedures especially on validating the key personnel and equipment to be used for the projects; and

OIC-City Engineer conduct test analysis of construction materials as mandated by Department of Public Works and Highways Department Order No. 11 series of 2017.

The Management, through the City Engineer conduct re-inspection and verification of the delivered or installed solar LED lights as to quantity and conformity with specifications based on the Program of Work. Thereafter, undertake appropriate action by demanding from the contractor the excess payment as a result of installing a 25w solar light instead of the required 300w.
The Management through the BAC refrain from engaging the services of an ineligible supplier and to plan ahead the procurement so that proper modality will be used.

Management through the BAC Secretariat and Public Information Officer post the procurement opportunities and other required information at the City’s official website and social media platforms and come up with other measures to ensure wide dissemination thereof and to attract other interested contractors.

Management through the OIC-City Engineer and GSO  immediately conduct an inventory of the materials and other items relating to these beautification projects and formulate an action plan which includes its safekeeping, future use, disposal and other measures.  Further, refrain from incurring huge amount of funds for decorations and find other alternatives to save government resources for other projects and activities.

Management direct the CT-TODA to formulate the guidelines regarding the operations of “E-Bikes” as part of an integrated transport system in the City and institutionalize the same through a City Ordinance to ensure the safety of the riding public and to generate additional income from its regulation.

Management to:

Submit the SP Resolutions authorizing the disbursements in the amount of ₱72,429,767.70; and


Submit the LDRRMFIP and the Reports on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF for CY 2022.

Management require the Human Resource Management Office to immediately conduct a study and design an organizational structure for CAO and CBO with due consideration as a department by expanding the existing office and creating additional positions to ensure that the staffing complement is aligned with the functions, performance targets, and programs of the City and to ensure efficient delivery of service.

The City Accountant  reconcile the records, retrieve relevant documents and coordinate with the City Treasurer for the return of the excess funds from completed programs/projects. 

The Management, on financial assistance which remained unutilized, to secure authority from the Source Agency to use these for other priority projects of the City.

Management direct the City Accountant to record the proposed Journal Entry for the liquidation on the utilized amount of the fund transfer from DILG.

Management  direct the City Accountant to carefully analyze and identify the cause/s of the abnormal balances of the identified accounts and effect the necessary adjustments to establish the correct balances in the SLs.

Management submit the lacking documents and a written justification/ explanation on the noted deficiencies, for the Audit Team’s verification and evaluation, to avoid disallowance in audit. Henceforth,  Management ensure that any disbursements of public funds are covered with appropriations and the documentary requirements particularly on the purchase of lot are strictly observed.

Management to: 

Refund the insurance premium paid totaling ₱52,890.57 for those buildings which were already demolished.

Management ensures a thorough revalidation of the listed ineligible beneficiaries.  In addition, should the revalidation prove that the listed persons are indeed ineligible, we henceforth recommended that the Local Chief Executive, the City Accountant and the City Social Welfare Development Officer send demand letters to the 2,629 ineligible beneficiaries for the immediate refund of the illegally received subsidy totaling ₱15,774,000.00.

Management, through the CSWDO, verify the status of the above beneficiaries and check whether they were disqualified from receiving the second tranche of the cash assistance.

Further, if these beneficiaries were still included in the 2nd tranche, CSWDO facilitate communication for the return of cash assistance received from the LGU.

Management, through the CSWDO, verify if these SAP recipients received the Pantawid grant and check whether they were disqualified from receiving the second tranche of the cash assistance if they had received both Pantawid and SAP subsidy for the month of April 2020.

Further, if these beneficiaries were still included in the 2nd tranche, the CSWDO facilitate communication for the return of cash assistance received from LGU.

Management submit a justification for undertaking the Emergency Procurement under the Bayanihan Act without complying with the requirements of GPPB Circular No. 01-2020 and to be always updated with the issuances of the GPPB as the governing board for all the procurement activities of the government.

Management to submit the lacking documents as required under GPPB Circular No. 01-2020 dated April 6, 2020. We also recommend that Management require the preparation of the MOA on payments of the same nature to establish proper utilization and monitoring of government funds.
Management submit the lacking required necessary documents to establish the validity of the transactions in pursuance to R.A. No. 9184 and COA Circular 2012-001.
Management submit a written explanation on the deficiencies noted and to submit the specified necessary documentary requirements pursuant to Section 4(6) of P.D. No. 1445.
Management submit an explanation or justification on the deficiencies noted for the Audit Team’s evaluation.

Management direct the City Treasurer to:

Facilitate the retrieval of vouchers and their supporting documents from the concerned personnel and submit them immediately to the Accounting office for appropriate recording and to avoid disallowance.

Management rescind the contract with CSWMI in accordance with the procedures laid down in the JVA, if there is any and in accordance with the provisions of the general laws and demand for the payment of damages that may be awarded under the contract and other pertinent laws.

Management to:

Direct the Accountant to record in the individual employees’ ledgers the amount of contributions/ deductions and reconcile the accounts and explain why the amount remitted differed from the amount withheld thus the incurrence of over remittance to GSIS and non- remittance to BIR, PAG-IBIG and PhilHealth of material amounts.

Management establish an Internal Audit Service office with sufficient support from the top Management to gain the cooperation/confidence of the auditee in compliance with AO No. 70, National Guidelines on Internal Control System (NGICS) and Section 124 of P.D. No. 1445 in order to strengthen accountability, ensure ethical, economical, efficient and effective operations, improve the quality and quantity of outputs and enable agencies to better respond to the requirements of the public they serve.

Management to:

Require the different market collectors to prepare, on a periodic basis, the Report on Unpaid Stall Rentals and submit to the City Treasurer/Accountant for monitoring/recording  purposes; and

Require the City Accountant that the total realizable income from market stall rental fees is properly taken up in the books of the City Government.

Management to direct the City Accountant to: 

Analyze all the receivables from NGOs/POs that are dormant, long outstanding and unsubstantiated for proper disposition, otherwise request for write-off;

Maintain SLs for receivables to monitor collections of loan amortizations from concerned NGOs/POs; and

Make representations with the Focal Persons to facilitate the liquidations thereof.

Management  direct the City Legal Officer, City Assessor, General Services Officer and other officials to facilitate and expedite the transfer of ownership of the lots in the name of the City to protect the interest of the government. Conduct an appraisal of all lots acquired through donations or transfers without cost and come up with their fair value;

Cause the Accountant to book the fair values of the lands, when available.
Management to account for the missing units to clear them of their accountability.
Direct the officials/employees found liable be made to refund the amount of ₱7,272,750.00.

We recommended and the Acting City Accountant agreed to review the depreciation schedule and prepare the necessary adjustments to correct the depreciation expense and the carrying value of the Buildings and other accounts for fair presentation in the financial statements.

We recommended and the Acting City Accountant agreed to:
a. Make the necessary adjusting entries to reflect the correct accounts balances, as follows:
Due to NGAs ₱3,949,843.63
Other Receivables ₱3,949,843.63
- For Talisay City Super Health Center
Due to NGAs 349,999.86
Other Receivables 349,999.86
- For Zone 3 Barangay Health Center
b. Recognize the Due to NGAs account for any subsequent actual fund transfers from national government agencies in accordance with the RCA for LGUs provided under COA Circular No. 2015-009, to present fairly the accounts in the financial statements.
We recommended and the Management agreed to discontinue the practice of charging prior years’ expenditures to the current appropriation and observe proper matching of expenses against appropriation.

We recommended and the Acting City Accountant agreed to prepare the necessary adjusting entries to correct the affected account balances as follows:
Construction in Progress –
₱11,766,412.28
Buildings and Other Structures
Road Networks ₱199,000.00
Buildings ₱11,965,412.28

We recommended and the Acting City Accountant agreed to reconcile the Buildings account balance per books with the subsidiary ledger to determine the details of the unreconciled amount totaling ₱26.635 million for any correction/adjustment.

We recommended and the Management agreed to release quarterly the RPT shares of the barangays, within five days following the end of each quarter in compliance with Section 271(d) of RA No. 7160 to fund the implementation of development programs, projects, and activities.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the GAD Focal Person:
a) Reconcile the inconsistencies between the endorsed GPB against the approved Annual Budget and submit to the DILG any changes on the succeeding GPB; and
b) Ensure submission of the annual GPB and the corresponding Accomplishment Report to the Audit Team within the prescribed period pursuant to Item V of COA Circular 2014-001.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the BAC Secretariat furnish the Audit Team a copy of all perfected contracts and purchase orders within five working days upon approval together with the supporting documents for review pursuant to the requirements of COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009.

We recommended that the City Mayor:
a. As Head of Procuring Entity (HoPE), require the BAC to strictly conduct the procurement process before the issuance of Purchase Order for the delivery of the procured equipment as provided in Sections 2, 10 and 52.1 of the 2016 RIRR of RA No. 9184 to ensure the propriety and legality on the payment of the purchased items;
b. Require the Receiving/Acceptance and Inspection Officers to refrain from receiving procured and delivered equipment without the Purchase Order completely filled-out with the terms and conditions and other required data and duly signed by the HoPE and supplier for conformity; and
c. As HoPE, and the BAC strictly procure the equipment thru competitive bidding and resort only to any of the alternative methods of procurement whenever justified by the conditions provided in Rule XVI to promote economy and efficiency and to ensure that the most advantageous price for the City Government is obtained.

We recommended that the City Mayor:
a. Require the BAC and other responsible officials to submit the remaining required documents on the Design and Build of Legacy Project of New Multi-Purpose Building and New Public Market and Terminal Complex projects for the full conduct of review on its compliance with the provisions of 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184; and
b. Direct the BAC Secretariat to improve their documents management and to ensure that all procurements undertaken by the City are properly documented.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the BAC to:
a. Submit the basis for adopting the Design and Build Scheme for the Construction of Legacy Project Multi-Purpose Building and New Public Market and Terminal Complex projects as provided by Section 5 of Annex G of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184; and
b. Ensure the existence of any conditions in Section 5 of Annex G of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 before recommending the use of Design and Build Scheme in the subsequent Infrastructure Projects.

We recommended and the Management agreed that:
a. The Bids and Awards Committee submit all contracts, variation orders, approved number of days’ extension; implementation schedule and other supporting documents as provided in Item 9.0 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 for full review on its compliance with 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184;
b. The City Planning and Development Coordinator (CPDC) and the City Engineer conduct close monitoring and evaluation of all infrastructure projects implemented by the City; and
c. The CPDC and the City Engineer thoroughly plan and expedite execution of infrastructure projects in succeeding periods based on the contract schedule.

We recommended and Management agreed to require the Inventory Committee to convene and strategize the implementation of the procedures in the one-time cleansing of all PPEs owned by the City as laid down in COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020. This is to resolve the recurring issues on the discrepancy between the property records and accounting records, and to come up with reliable PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability.

We recommended that the City Accountant determine  and record the depreciation expense in the books of accounts to fairly present the value of the PPE and other affected accounts in the financial statements.

Further, we recommended the City Accountant to prepare and maintain a complete lapsing schedule for all PPE accounts.

We recommended that Management, through the City Treasurer and the City Accountant, trace the discrepancies between the cash books and the Cash-Local Treasury and CIB-LCCA accounts. We further recommended that Management henceforth conduct properly the monthly reconciliation between cash records as against accounting records.

We recommended and Management through the City Treasurer agreed to reach out to the payees for verification, so that appropriate action can be taken, such as issuing a replacement check if it is necessary.

Further, we recommended and the City Accountant agreed to make the necessary JEV to effect the  adjustment following the proposed adjusting journal entry as follows:
Cash in Bank			₱675,898.11
Accounts Payable			₱675,898.11

We recommended that Management direct the City Accountant to immediately stop the practice of recording purchases of inventories as outright expense, and instead record them  first under inventory account even if directly issued to end-users.

We recommended and the Management, through the City Accountant, agreed to:
Analyze and conduct a thorough review of the dormant accounts payable, retrieve pertinent supporting documents, and check/validate the existence of actual claimants by sending confirmations prior to the reversion of these outstanding payables. If the transactions cannot be substantiated with the necessary documents and the payable accounts are found to be non-existent due to the absence of valid claimants, the funds intended for said obligations be adjusted and reverted to the unappropriated surplus in accordance with the pertinent provisions of P.D. No. 1445; and

Effect the necessary adjusting entries to reclassify other payables erroneously recorded in the AP following the proposed adjusting journal entry:
Accounts Payable			₱24,804,046.27
Other Payables			₱24,804,046.27
We recommended and Management, through the City Accountant, agreed to conduct a thorough review of the deductions for GSIS premiums/loan amortization of the personnel.

We recommended that the Management direct the City Accountant to:
Effect the necessary adjusting entry to reclassify the shares from PAGCOR erroneously recorded as income following the proposed journal entry:
Share from PAGCOR (GF)		`	₱2,500,000.00
Due to GOCCs (TF)			₱2,500,000.00
Recognize in the appropriate account the funds received with specific and intended purpose and use the funds appropriately in accordance with the guidelines and law.

We recommended that Management, through the City Accountant, reclassify the prizes from Due to LGUs to Prior Period Adjustment (Miscellaneous Income) and coordinate with the City Treasurer to include these as a new funding source subject to budgetary laws and regulations.

We recommended that the Management direct the City Accountant to:
Effect the necessary adjusting entries to reclassify Other Maintenance and Operating Expenses erroneously recorded as Advances for Payroll following the proposed adjusting journal entry:
Prior Period Adjustment			   ₱445,428.00
Advances for Payroll 				₱445,428.00

Review the SLs of the Disbursing Officers and make necessary adjusting entries in the SL to reflect the correct SL balances.

We recommended and Management agreed to direct the City Accountant to facilitate the preparation and the filing of request for write-off for dormant accounts  following the documentary requirements and procedures prescribed under COA Circular No. 2023-008, amending COA Circular No. 2016-005.

We recommended that the BAC and BAC Secretariat ensure compliance with the provision of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 particularly the (i) thorough post-qualification procedures especially on validating the key personnel and equipment to be used for the projects; (ii) timeliness in the execution of contract agreement; (iii) timeliness in the conduct of bidding prior to date of celebration  (iv) submission of proof of invitation of two observers and COA representative in all stages of the procurement process

We reiterated our recommendation that Management, through the BAC Secretariat and/or in coordination with Public Information Office, post the procurement opportunities and other required information at the City’s official website as well as in social media platforms and come up with other measures to ensure wide dissemination thereof to attract other interested contractors.

We recommended that the Management stop the charging of expenditures that are not fully aligned with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-002 to avoid depleting the funds intended for disaster management, prevention, and recovery.

We recommended that Management, through the City Budget Office (CBO) and LDRRM Office, provide a written explanation on charging the subject expenditures to LDRRMF notwithstanding the nature of such expenditures, which are not directly related to disaster risk reduction and management activities, for the Audit Team’s evaluation. Henceforth, we recommended that Management properly assess the nature of the expenses to determine if these can be charged against the LDRRMF.
We recommended and Management, through the City Accountant and other concerned officials, agreed to submit the lacking documents to facilitate further evaluation of the transactions.

We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee submit a written explanation on why the above-mentioned procurements were not consolidated and procured through competitive public bidding as required under R.A. No. 9184. Henceforth, properly plan and consolidate the City’s procurement requirements pursuant to Section 7 of R.A. No. 9184.

We also recommended that the BAC provide an explanation for awarding the contract on the procurement of tent to a supplier whose business, according to the submitted business permit, is into rental services.

We recommended and Management, through the City Treasurer, agreed to intensify its tax collection campaign for RPTs and if warranted, enforce the appropriate remedies as provided in R.A. No. 7160 or in the City’s Revenue Code.

We recommended and Management agreed to prioritize the revision and updating of the 2011 Revenue Code of the City, pursuant to the provisions of the LGC, to increase local revenues and receipts and enhance the City’s budget for improved government services.

We recommended that Management, through the City Treasurer and the City Accountant, ensure the timely release of the barangays’ shares from RPT collections in compliance with Section 271(d) of R.A. No. 7160.

We recommended that Management look for an available area in the City Hall premises that can be used as warehouse or stockroom.  If not feasible, construct a new structure for inventory safekeeping so that centralized inventory keeping and management will be implemented and proper inventory processes will be followed using the procedures laid down in Section 122, Chapter 7, Volume 1 of NGAS for LGUs.

To mitigate the risks pending the availability of the stockroom, we further recommended that Management direct the designated supply officers in each department to prepare and maintain stock cards, and to submit a monthly inventory report reflecting the items used and the remaining balance. This report should be validated and consolidated by the GSO as a control measure for inventory usage and procurement.

We recommended that henceforth, Management return unspent balances to the SA, or request approval from the SA to transfer the unexpended balance to the GF if the disposition thereof is not provided in the MOA, Trust Agreement or Memorandum of Understanding, and close the respective bank account maintained to avoid incurrence of possible dormancy fees/charges in the ensuing year.

We recommended that Management, through the City Accountant, regularly prepare the monthly BRS separately for each of the existing depository accounts of the City to facilitate reconciliation between the book and bank balances of all accounts as at a given period.

We recommended that the City Mayor, as Chairperson of the Local School Board (LSB), require the DepEd Talisay City Division Superintendent to submit the consolidated Priority Lists of SLR supported by copy of the duly accomplished market scoping and evaluation tools submitted by the SLR committees for the evaluation by the Audit Team.

We recommended and Management agreed to ensure proper monitoring of the fund, and adhere to the guidelines set forth by the LBC on the unutilized portion, as well as on the reporting and posting requirements.

We recommended and Management, through the City Accountant, agreed to submit to the Audit Team the lacking documents required under the pertinent provisions of the Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184, and COA Circular No. 2012-001, for the Audit Team’s evaluation.

We recommended and Management agreed to assign a BAC Secretariat member who will prepare the PMR of the City and comply with the posting requirement of the APP and PMR to promote transparency and public monitoring of the City’s procurement process.

We recommended and Management, through the City Accountant agreed to observe the following measures:
Reconciliation of the recorded taxes withheld against the actual remittances to identify the breakdown of the unremitted balance and cause the remittance to BIR once reconciled or identified;
Conduct of monthly reconciliation of the Due to BIR account to prevent undue accumulation of unremitted balances; and
Immediate remittance of all taxes withheld to avoid further penalties.

We recommended and Management, through the City Accountant, agreed to identify the causes and details of the unreconciled amount, including whether it pertains to previously or currently withheld premium contributions that were left unremitted. The identified amount should be promptly remitted, and take up the necessary adjustments based on the reconciliation results.

We recommended and Management, through the Payroll In-Charge, agreed to conduct a thorough review of the deductions of the GSIS premiums/loan amortization of the personnel and ensure that these amounts are remitted intact and in full to the GSIS within the prescribed period.

We recommended and Management, through the City Accountant, agreed to reconcile the remaining unreconciled portion and make the necessary adjustments or remit the amount to the Pag-IBIG Fund, as determined during reconciliation. Additionally, a thorough review of personnel deductions related to Pag-IBIG premiums and loan amortizations shall be conducted to ensure timely and complete remittance within the prescribed period.

We recommended and the City Accountant– Designate agreed to prepare the necessary accounting entries to record the completed project to the PPE or Parks, Plaza and Monuments account under the General Fund books and provide the corresponding depreciation expense in accordance with IPSAS No. 17 based on its useful life.

We recommended and the City Accountant– Designate agreed to prepare the necessary adjusting entries to reflect the correct balances of the liability and equity accounts by debiting the Other Payables account and crediting Prior Period Adjustment Errors. Thereafter, discontinue the practice of preparing a corollary entry for the recognition of liquidation in advance because corollary entries are not being used under the new accounting system.

We recommended and the Management agreed that: a) The City Accountant– Designate apply the perpetual inventory method of recording purchases of goods as inventories and recognize the corresponding expenses for actual issuances; b) The City Accountant – Designate prepare an adjusting entry to reflect the correct balance of inventory account at year-end; and c) The GSO maintain updated Inventory records and conduct periodic physical inventory of supplies or property pursuant to Section 124 of MNGAS for LGUs.

We recommended and the City Accountant agreed to immediately record all reconciling items in the year of incurrence to reflect reliable Cash-in-Bank balance in the financial statements as of year-end pursuant to Section 3.3 of COA Circular No. 96-011 dated October 2, 1996.

We recommended and Management agreed that the City Accountant prepare adjusting journal entries to restore the liabilities of the City to the corresponding creditors by debiting the Cash in Bank account and crediting the affected liability account.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the CPDC and the City Engineer and all other implementing offices of the City expedite the implementation of all projects funded by the 20 percent Development Fund including its supplemental budget, to ensure optimum utilization of the fund so that the services and benefits from these projects are delivered to the constituents without unnecessary delay.

We recommended and the City Mayor agreed to: a) Direct the BAC and the City Engineer to submit the appropriate supporting documents to prove that the projects were implemented and completed and the validity of the payments pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012; b) Instruct the Inspectorate Team to examine and inspect thoroughly the accomplishments of the JO workers/contractors before and after the execution of the projects and provide attachments/documents to prove that the projects are implemented through the submission of pictures; c) Require the City Accountant to thoroughly verify/scrutinize supporting documents attached to the disbursement vouchers before payments are made and the Implementing Office ensure the completeness of the project in accordance with the program of works before recommending the payment of the work accomplished; and d) Henceforth, submit to the Audit Team copies of the Job Order contract within five working days from the execution of the contract pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009.

We recommended and the Management agreed that: a) The City Treasurer determine the 20 percent share of the income generated from building permit fees and other charges since CY 2013 and remit the five percent DPWH share to the account of the DPWH Secretary under Special Account No. 154 thru the Bureau of Treasury; and b) The City Building Official utilize the 15 percent share of the City for the operating and incidental expenses related to the functions of the Building Permit Approval Section by preparing a Program of Work detailing the manner the 15 percent share would be utilized subject to the approval by the Local Chief Executive.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the City Assessor prepare the updated schedule of market values for the different classes of real property situated in the City and conduct a general revision of real property assessment every three years pursuant to the provisions of Sections 212 and 219 of RA No. 7160.

We recommended and the City Accountant– Designate agreed to review the composition of the Road Networks and Construction in Progress account and make the necessary adjusting entries including the computation of depreciation expense of road networks not yet provided with depreciation to reflect the correct balances of the affected accounts pursuant to the Revised Chart of Accounts for LGUs per COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015. We also recommend that the Budget Officer be briefed on the correct accounting entries to take up transactions involving fixed assets.

We recommended and the City Accountant– Designate agreed to review the composition of the Due to NGAs account under the Trust Fund books and make the necessary accounting entries to transfer the completed projects as PPE or Assets under the General Fund and provide the corresponding depreciation expense in accordance with IPSAS No. 17.

We recommended and the City Mayor agreed to direct the City Accountant- Designate to: a) Submit to the Auditor the monthly BRS of the three Land Bank of the Philippines (LBP) accounts and ensure that monthly BRS of all bank accounts are submitted to the Audit Team within the prescribed period; and b) Review prior year’s reconciling items, secure supporting documents, and immediately record in the books the appropriate adjustments to reflect reliable cash-in-bank balances.

We recommended and the Inventory Committee agreed to: a) Prepare the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) for all unserviceable motor vehicles and submit the same to the Appraisal Committee for the determination of the appraised value, and prepare a report thereon; and b) After the appraised values are determined, immediately file an application for disposal of the unserviceable vehicles, thru the City Mayor, to the Audit Team Leader for inspection and determination whether the subject property is with value or without value pursuant to Art. 444 of the Rules and Regulations Implementing the Local Government Code of 1991.
observations.reason: No IIRUP submitted as of writing.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the City Mayor agreed to ensure that all motor vehicles owned by the City are properly and timely registered with the LTO and are insured with the GSIS to avoid additional expenses in terms of penalties and payment of damages or third-party liabilities in case of vehicular accidents or fortuitous events.

We recommend and the City Mayor agreed to: a) Direct the Acting City Accountant-designate to maintain a special account for the 20 percent DF by maintaining a complete set of subsidiary ledgers under the GF Proper for easy monitoring of the receipt, transfer and utilization and to enable the generation of the financial statements which can aid in decision-making and other purposes; and b) Require the City Development Council to identify priority development projects and programs to be charged against the ₱154.583 million unencumbered/unobligated 20% DF.

We reiterated our recommendation that the City Mayor and the City Accountant: a) Enforce immediate settlement of all outstanding cash advances totaling ₱3,207,524.25, especially those intended for special purposes pursuant to Item 4.4.2 of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997; and b) Otherwise, effect the withholding of their salaries pursuant to Item 5.1.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002 until such time that the cash advances are fully settled.
observations.reason: There were still outstanding prior years’ cash advances intended for special purposes as of writing.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City of Talisay submit specific law, other statutory authority, or rules and regulations of competent authority expressly authorizing such payment of additional benefit to its officials and employees pursuant to COA Circular No. 2013-003 dated January 30, 2013. Otherwise, such payment could be considered wanting of legal basis.
observations.reason: The City submitted explanation but could not provide supporting legal basis as of writing.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor and/or the City Accountant immediately send a follow up letter to COA-ICFAU to ascertain the status of previously submitted liquidation documents for charges against confidential fund for CYs 2004-2013 and request issuance of credit advice.
observations.reason: Outstanding Balance as of December 31, 2024 is ₱6,207,660.00; demand letters were sent.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor propose for approval to the Sangguniang Panlungsod specific guidelines on the implementation of the Assistance to Individuals in Crisis Situation using as basis the guidelines issued by the DSWD under DSWD Memorandum Circular No. 011.
observations.reason: Sangguniang Panlungsod Resolution indicating the guidelines was submitted to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City of Talisay submit specific law, other statutory authority, or rules and regulations of competent authority expressly authorizing such payment of additional benefit to its officials and employees pursuant to COA Circular No. 2013-003 dated January 30, 2013. Otherwise, such payment could be considered wanting of legal basis.
observations.reason: City submitted explanation but did not provide a specific law as of writing.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City officials who are liable for the disallowance to settle the amount of disallowance immediately to the Cashier. Further, we recommended that the City Mayor direct the Cashier/Treasurer to withhold payment of any money due such persons liable pursuant to Sections 3, Rule XIII of the 2009 Revised Rules and Procedures of the Commission on Audit.
observations.reason: Deductions were made from payroll for those still in service; others no longer connected to LGU.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Treasurer/Market Supervisor to: 1. Revoke the Lease Agreement of those delinquent block owners and award the market blocks to other interested parties; 2. Ensure that the terms and conditions as embodied in the Lease Agreement are strictly enforced; and 3. Propose for the amendments of some provisions of the Market Ordinance of 2012 to include granting certain options to those who could not fully pay their outstanding accounts.
observations.reason: Lease agreements of delinquent block owners were not revoked; Market is in process of preparing amendments for market ordinance.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the OIC- City Accountant to recognize or record the receivables from home partners for the cost of the lot awarded to them once all the documents are established and send demand letters to all Bayanihan home partners with overdue amortization.
observations.reason: Demand letters were sent; receivables not yet recorded in the books.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor submit specific legal basis for the grant of Economic Welfare Assistance to the City officials and employees for CY 2017. Henceforth, he must ensure that benefits or incentives granted to City officials and employees are expressly provided by law, and other statutory authority and the rules and regulations promulgated by competent authority pursuant to COA Circular No. 2013-003 dated January 13, 2013.
observations.reason: City submitted explanation but legal basis was not provided to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management demand payments of the loans granted to cooperatives and closely monitor compliance of their commitments, otherwise, institute legal action to collect the same, if warranted.
observations.reason: Demand letters were sent to the farmers; some cooperatives were paying; others dissolved.

We recommended and Management agreed that the:
BAC explain the above-noted lapses and ensure that the BAC conducts thorough post-qualification procedures especially on validating the key personnel and equipment to be used for the projects; and
OIC-City Engineer conduct test analysis of construction materials as mandated by DPWH DO No. 11 series of 2017.
During the exit conference, the BAC Chairperson explained that the conduct of the post-qualification activities was shortened since the submission of the work program and the detailed activities to be done were sometimes delayed.  The BAC was also presumptive that the winning bidder are capable and well-known based on their performance from other agencies.
The Audit Team emphasized the need for observance of the guidelines of RA No. 9184.  It is highly improbable to complete the process in three days as there is a timeline for each process which cannot be dispensed with. Also, the bidding process is non-discretionary, thus the post-qualification is very important. The Audit Team further recommends to plan ahead should there be activities that will be undertaken by the City.

We recommended that the Management, through the OIC-City Engineer, conduct re-inspection and verification of the delivered or installed solar LED lights as to quantity and conformity with specifications based on the Program of Work. Thereafter, undertake appropriate action by demanding from the contractor the excess payment as a result of installing a 25w solar light instead of the required 300w.
The OIC-City Engineer explained that the office intended to purchase solar lights that can produce up to 300 watts, in which they have indicated in all procurement documents, however, the true rated power of the product delivered was only 25 watts, with maximum wattage of 600. They acknowledged the error in the specification and fully understand the importance of accurate documentation. They are also amenable on the price difference mentioned in the audit observation, however based on further canvassing from other suppliers, the current market trend for solar LED lights varies based on the true rated power, with higher-rated power lights commanding a higher price. As to the result of the Audit Team’s inspection on the project, the installed 25 watts is recommended for 6 meters pole which requires spacing at least 10 meters apart, however, the existing posts is approximately 20 meters apart. Hence, for them to recommend higher wattage of lights, it needs to replace the existing poles.
We acknowledge the comment of the OIC-City Engineer, however, it is imperative to note that the discrepancy between the specification indicated in the procurement documents as against the actual delivered items is not a mere error in documentation.  It is to be emphasized that the specification in the documents presented from the requisition, the item quoted by the supplier and up to payment was the 300w.  Simply put, what the agency paid for should have been the items received or delivered. This variance has led to a significant price difference amounting to ₱234,000.00, as computed by the Audit Team using the specification found online for the same brand, which is disadvantageous to the City Government. Hence, we maintain our stand that Management undertakes necessary steps to demand from the contractor the price difference as a result of installing 25w instead of the originally specified 300w.

We recommended that Management through the BAC refrain from engaging the services of an ineligible supplier and to plan ahead the procurement so that proper modality will be used.
The BAC Chairperson explained that the GPPB in its approved guidelines through GPPB Resolution No. 03-2020 has simplified the government procurement process, wherein, the HOPE may authorize the end user unit to directly negotiate with legitimate suppliers. Procuring entities are allowed to apply the rules on Negotiated Procurement (Emergency Case) during a state of calamity.  Moreover, the procurement of the cards and forms cannot be lumped because the procurement depends on the needs and necessities of the respective rural health units of the City.
During the exit conference, the Audit Team emphasized that based on the documents, the dates in the Purchase Requests (PR) were made at a close interval. It could have been lumped because the procurement is just a matter of reproduction of the form. The procurement could have been planned ahead so that the proper mode of procurement would be resorted to.

We recommended and Management through the BAC agreed to complete the necessary information required in the preparation of APP and PMR by following the prescribed format of GPPB and have it regularly submitted to the Audit Team for review and verification.

We recommended and Management through the BAC Secretariat and Public Information Officer agreed to post the procurement opportunities and other required information at the City’s official website and social media platforms and come up with other measures to ensure wide dissemination thereof and to attract other interested contractors.
The BAC Chairperson explained that they tried to address this concern by creating a temporary website last year.  However, due to technical issues, the website was not running consistently therefore not available for advertisements and posting of invitations to bid. Despite the unavailability of the City’s official website, the BAC substantially complied with Section 21.2.1 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 that requires Invitation to Bid/Request for Expression of Interest to be posted at any conspicuous places on the premises of the Procuring Entity for seven calendar days and posting continuously in the PhilGEPS website.  The BAC Chairperson will also request the Public Information Office and the City HDRMO to open the positions for IT personnel to handle and maintain the City’s official website to ensure the widest dissemination of bid opportunities.

We recommended that Management refrain from displaying the pictures, image, color motif or motto associated with public officials on government PPAs pursuant to COA Circular No. 2013-004 and DILG MC No. 2010-101.
The BAC Chairperson explained that the motto “Aksyon Agad” printed on the cover of the journals does not mean that the Local Chief Executive monopolizes the motto to himself as a politician, rather, it is a bandwagon propaganda of good local governance where all public employees in the local government of Talisay are encouraged to do their duties and exercise their functions more efficiently and constituent-centered system of governance.
The Audit Team acknowledge the noble intention of the Local Chief Executive, however, the Audit Team maintains its stand that Management refrain from displaying any motto associated with the public officials on government PPAs pursuant to the existing guidelines.

We recommended and the City Mayor, as Chairperson of the LSB, agreed to prioritize the implementation of projects that will address the shortage in school facilities and instructional materials, and to coordinate with DepEd Talisay City Division Officials regarding the budget for MOOE that should be shouldered by DepEd rather than the City.
The Schools Division Superintendent explained that the Talisay City Division has a total of 44 public schools that needed the security services and utility workers. Also, calendar years 2021 to 2022 were under the pandemic period, when classes were mostly in Alternative Delivery Mode (ADM).  Printing of “modules” was of top priority for DepEd at that time, hence, the highest allocation in the Annual Budget. By the same reason, the LSB, could not prioritize repair and construction projects or procurement of instructional materials in schools because of quarantine restrictions and the uncertainty if the same restrictions would be back after being lifted.
During the exit conference, the Honorable City Mayor explained that they were able to put-up classrooms, school buildings and had purchased some books. For this year’s SEF budget, the City is about to conduct phase 2 and complete five new school buildings, and removed the lease of printers.

We recommended and the City Mayor, as Chairperson of the LSB, agreed to require the DepEd Talisay City Division Superintendent to submit the following:
Consolidated List of Requested SRMs of the Division which shall be supported by school inventory of SRMs, list of usage of new SRMs needed by the school and the study on how the SRMs will support the curriculum implementation; and
Approval from the Office of the Secretary regarding the procurement of SRMs.
22.7 	The Chairperson of the LSB explained that the most recent DepEd DO No. 035, series of 2019 entitled “2019 List of Approved Supplementary Learning Resources” did not require the submission and approval of the SRM to DepEd Secretary as compared to DO No. 53.  The Chairperson justified that the procured Math window cards from Caneo Enterprise do not fall within the purview of DO No. 035 but these are vital and important learning resources for DepEd that was procured through the regular mode of procurement. On the other hand, items procured from Rex Bookstore were technically not in the 2019 approved list of SLRs but these are needed for the senior high.
22.8  	The Audit Team maintains its stand that DepEd DO No. 53 is still applicable as  DO No. 035, series of 2019, which was mentioned in the comment of the Chairperson of the LSB  has been issued to provide guidelines on how to purchase SLRs for library hubs (Batches 7 and 8) and school libraries using the 2019 List of Approved SLRs.  Hence, the submission of Consolidated List of Requested SRMs of the Division which shall be supported by school inventory of SRMs, list of usage of new SRMs needed by the school and the study on how the SRMs will support the curriculum implementation is still required including the approval from the Office of the Secretary regarding the procurement of SRMs.

We recommended and Management agreed to require the:
City Accountant to review carefully the request for cash advances for salaries, honorarium and other similar payments to ensure that the amount to be cash advanced is equal to the net amount of payroll for a pay period;
City Treasurer and the City Administrator to stop the practice of transferring cash advances to another officers or employees.  Disbursement and liquidations are done by the person who did the cash advance; and
City Accountant to direct the officials and employees who have unliquidated cash advances to liquidate immediately, otherwise, withhold their salary pursuant to Item 5.1.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002.
During the exit conference, Management gave the following comments:
On the excessive grants, they explained that the requests for payroll forwarded from the department heads at times contain errors and were not deducted with withholding tax. Financial assistance for the Senior Citizens and PWDs also includes claims for those who were already deceased during the quarter. The Management agreed to maintain an updated list of Senior Citizens and PWDs and to finalize the payroll and honorarium prior to payment so that the exact amount will be cash advanced or the excess will be minimized.  Also, the Management agreed to shorten the liquidation period should there be excess in the cash advance.
On the transfer of cash advance from one Accountable Officer to another, Management commented that they aimed for a more efficient distribution of financial assistance to almost 15,000 senior citizens and PWDs, some were even bedridden.  Considering this and the City’s lack of manpower will lengthen the distribution thus, the transfer of cash to the barangay treasurers. The Management agreed to implement certain mechanisms to include the scheduling of the distribution of financial assistance to the beneficiaries.

We recommended and Management through the OIC-City Engineer and GSO agreed to immediately conduct an inventory of the materials and other items relating to these beautification projects and formulate an action plan which includes its safekeeping, future use, disposal and other measures in order that the City could get a return from these.  Further, refrain from incurring huge amount of funds for decorations and find other alternatives to save government resources for other projects and activities.
The City Administrator explained that an inventory was already conducted by the General Services Office and the Office of the City Engineer. The decorations lasted for months and the same were utilized as decorations during the Charter Day celebration, Valentines Day and Summer Trade Fair. Those that are no longer suitable for re-use especially materials that are susceptible to ordinary wear and tear like cloth and styrofoam were properly disposed of.  Materials and accessories like metals were reused by the Engineering Office and the Aksyon Agad team. Lightings that are still functioning are properly stored by the GSO.  As to the spending of “huge” funds for the decorations, taken into consideration the costs of materials for visual elements these days, it is undeniable that cost is a critical component in bringing the innovative ideas to life. The cost is reasonable as the decorations lasted even after the holiday season and being highlighted during the significant events of the City which draws people to city hall compound.

We recommended and the Management agreed to the following:

Ensure that the necessary supporting documents are attached to the disbursement voucher containing the defined scope of work of trainers and the accomplishment report to support the propriety of payment for services rendered; and

Limit the hiring of personnel under Job Order contracts to those extremely necessary.

Require the City Development Council (CDC) to prepare and include specific program, projects and activities that partakes the nature of investments and capital expenditures in the 20% Development Fund Plan pursuant to Item 2.3 of JMC No. 2017-01 dated                    February 22, 2017; and
Secure first approval or authorization from the Local Sanggunian prior to implementation of programs/ projects/activities funded out of the lumpsum appropriation under the 20% Development Fund pursuant to COA Memorandum No. 2010-014.

Likewise, ensure that the required AO’s certification at the end of each month in the Collector’s Cashbooks are complied with.

Effect immediately the necessary adjusting entries to reflect the correct account balances of the RPT/SET Receivables and the corresponding Deferred RPT/SET Income accounts; and
Prospectively, ensure strict adherence with the pro-forma journal entries provided in the MNGAS to avoid discrepancies/differences/variances in the balances of the accounts.

If all possible remedies by the Management to collect the receivables and to demand liquidation of CAs and fund transfers have been exhausted and documented but failed to collect, then proceed in procedures enumerated in Section 8.0 of COA Circular No. 2016-005; prepare a request for authority to write-off governed by the 2009 Revised Rules of Procedures of the COA (RRPC) and Sections 9.0 and 10.0 of the same Circular.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. create a separate Inventory Committee for one-time cleansing of PPE as required
under COA Circular No. 2020-006 and approve the PIP necessary for the conduct
and complete physical inventory;
b. direct the Inventory Committee to assigned Property Number (PN) for each
PPE items and incorporate in the report such PN;
c. require the Inventory Committee to include the Construction in Progress in the
conduct of annual physical count of properties and reflect the result of physical
count in the RPCPPE;
d. instruct the duly created Inventory Committee, the Municipal General
Services Office and Accounting Office to reconcile inventory count per
RPCPPE with property and accounting records;
e. direct the Municipal Accountant to reclassify the semi-expendable items totaling
P2,308,337.05 to their respective inventory and ensure that all small value assets
with useful life of more than one year but costing less than P15,000.00 are
properly classified and recorded accordingly and to prepare journal entries and
effect the necessary adjustments on the PPE, Accumulated Depreciation and
Government Equity accounts for a fair presentation of the accounts in the
financial statements;
f. instruct the GSO to issue the corresponding Inventory Custodian Slip (ICS) to all
the end-users of the 312 semi-expendable items and to cancel the Property
Acknowledgement Receipt (PAR) previously issued; and
g. create a Disposal and Appraisal Committee that will (i) inspect unserviceable
equipment and property; (ii) set the final appraised value of all disposable
properties; (iii) recommend the disposal to the Local Chief Executive for his
approval and the manner of disposal; and (iv) forward the approved documents to
the Municipal Accountant for the necessary adjustments in the PPE or for de-
recognition of the unserviceable properties totaling P48,903,194.19 in the books
for a more reliable balances of PPE accounts in the financial statements.
We further recommended that the Municipality follow the guidelines set forth in COA
Circular No. 2020-006 for the one-time cleansing of PPE accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
a. correctly classify the outstanding loans in the non-current liabilities as Loans
Payable – Domestic account in the Statement of Financial Position for a fair
presentation of the accounts in the financial statements; and
b. disclose all information pertaining to the long-term loan to enhance the value of
the report of financial data in the financial statements.
Henceforth, adhere strictly to COA Circular 2015-009 in the use of the prescribed
accounts in the recording of transactions to ensure accuracy and reliability of the
accounts reported in the financial statements.

We reiterated that the Municipal Mayor require the
§ Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to:
a. Make necessary action to inform and demand the personnel about their
obligations to the municipality and cause the immediate refund/restitution of
the cash shortage and collection of the amount due from municipal officials
and employees representing unpaid personal telephone bills and bank loans;
b. Regularly send demand letters and statement of accounts receivables to
the listed debtors in order for them to settle their obligation;
c. Initiate appropriate legal actions to recover from officials and employees
who were already separated from the service;
d. Intensify collections of Loans and Other Receivables, designate key
personnel who will monitor the loans/assistance granted and enforce
payment thereof. Otherwise, resort to appropriate legal remedies; and
e. Coordinate with DepED personnel and exhaust all possible means to
reconstruct or locate the liquidation documents/transmittal of records
submitted by them in support to their unliquidated cash advances.
§ Municipal Accountant
a. Verify and locate the source documents for the recorded Advances to
Officers and Employees of P378,672.03 as basis for posting to subsidiary
records; and
b. Analyze the accounts that are dormant and long outstanding and make
necessary actions for their proper disposition in compliance with COA
Circular No. 2016-005.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require:
a) Municipal Accountant to present or submit the lacking documents and ensure
that all documentary requirements for the grant of financial assistance are
complied with before certifying as to completeness of the supporting documents
in the disbursement vouchers; and
b) Municipal Social Welfare Officer to:
§ submit lacking documents such as photos/pictures of damage houses and
assessment reports or explanation/justification letter on the basis of their
assessments; and
§ conduct thorough assessment (social case study report) of the situation and
qualifications of the individuals seeking for financial assistance before making
any recommendation for the payment of financial assistance.

We recommended that the Municipal School Board improve SEF utilization
performance by maximizing the use of available funds to implement various
programs, projects and activities allowed to be charged against the SEF under the
existing laws, rules and regulations.
We recommended that the management strictly adhere to the provisions of Section of
4(6) of PD No. 1445 and Section 1.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 and attached the
required supporting documents for the cash advances/payment for wages of JO
personnel.

We recommended and the management agreed that Municipal Mayor direct the
Municipal Accountant to:
a. observe the proper procedure in recording the receipt of supplies to the
appropriate Inventory accounts instead of directly recording the same as
expense for fair presentation of asset and expense accounts in the books; and
b. thereafter, comply strictly to the provisions of Item 17 and 44 of the IPSAS 12.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. instruct the Municipal Development Council, Municipal Planning and Development
Office, Municipal Engineering Office and other Offices concerned in the
implementation of projects under the 20 per cent DF to monitor and evaluate the
implementation of all 20 per cent DF programs/projects/activities to achieve the
benefits fully desired to be derived therefrom in compliance with DBM-DOF-DILG
JMC No. 2020-1; and
b. direct concerned Officials of the Municipality to observe the guidelines on the
appropriation and utilization of 20 per cent DF under DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No.
2020-1.
We recommended and the management agreed that the Municipal Mayor require
the Municipal General Services and Engineering Office to submit copy of
contracts, together with its supporting documents, to COA within five working
days after their perfection and/or approval as provided under COA Circular No.
2009-001 dated February 12, 2009, for the timely review and inspection.
We recommended that the Municipality of Talisay demand for liquidated
damages from erring contractors and and enforce strictly and sternly the
penalties provided under Paragraph 8.1 of Annex E of the 2016 Revised IRR of
RA No. 9184 on the incurrence of delays in the completion of government
procurement contracts in order to safeguard government resources as well as
the interest of all citizenry.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Engineering Office to:
a. perform sufficient planning and detailed engineering activities and ensure
that issues such as site problems, concerns by local/national government
units and other matters that could delay project implementation are
resolved prior to procurement of infrastructure contracts; and
b. ensure that projects are completed within the contract time to avoid delays
resulting in deferred benefits to the public apart from the incurrence of
liquidating damages.
We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) direct the
contractor to post the required warranty security for the four infrastructure
projects.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure completeness
posting of warranty security for completed infrastructure projects before
processing payments to contractors.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. BAC to submit justification on the noted observation and submit
immediately all the lacking documents enumerated in Annex K to the
Office of the Auditor for verification and audit;
b. Municipal Accountant to ensure the completeness of the supporting
documents as required under Section 4 (6) of PD No. 1445 and Section
9.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 before processing payments to
contractors;
and
Henceforth, comply strictly with Section 4 (6) of PD No. 1445 and Section 9.1 of
COA Circular No. 2012-001.

We recommended and the management agreed that the Municipal Disaster Risk
Reduction and Mitigation Office (MDRRMO) monitor and evaluate the utilization
of LDRRMF based on the programs, projects and activities listed in the Local
Disaster Risk Reduction and Mitigation Fund Investment Plan (LDRRMFIP) to
ensure the full implementation of the pre-disaster preparedness and response
activities of the Municipality.
We also recommended strict compliance to Sections 2(e) and 12(6) of RA No. 10121 to
strengthen the capacity of the Municipality to build a disaster resilient community.
We recommended and the management agreed that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. MDRRMO to prepare and submit the LDRRMFIP to the Office of the Auditor
in the prescribed format within the time frame provided; and
b. Municipal Accountant to ensure correctness of the information presented in
the Monthly Report on the Sources and Utilization of the LDRRMF.
Henceforth, strictly adhere to the guidelines set forth in COA Circular No. 2012-002.
We recommended and the management agreed that the Municipal Mayor require
the Municipal Budget Officer to strictly comply with Section 21 of RA No. 10121
by including in their yearly budget the statutory requirement of at least five
percent (5%) of the total appropriations for LDRRMF.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
make the necessary journal entry to present the correct year-end balances of the
affected accounts in the financial statements of the Municipality. Henceforth,
record under the Trust Liabilities-DRRMF account the DRRM funds received
from National Government.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
a. GAD Focal Person to:
· conduct regular monitoring on the implementation of GAD Plan and Budget to
ensure full utilization of the fund; and
· prepare the annual GAD Plan and Budget and its Accomplishment Report in
the required format for the proper evaluation of the implementation of GAD
Plan and Budget and submit the same within the prescribed period.
b. Municipal Budget Officer to include in their yearly budget the statutory
requirement of at least five per cent (5%) of the total appropriations for GAD.

We recommended that Management:
Ensure that funds received for specific purpose(s) or for specific program(s) and project(s), such as the LGSF-COVID-19 FA to LGUs and other similar grants/financial assistance to LGUs, should be utilized only according to such purpose(s) or such program(s) and project(s). Moreover, applicable rules and regulations on the utilization of such funds should be faithfully adhered to;
Comply those laws, rules, and regulations pertaining to particular transactions such as, but not limited to, the acquisition of motor vehicle and procurement of goods intended for distribution wherein certain supporting documents (such as APMV and RDS, respectively) are required, in order to establish its regularity, should be observed strictly, without prejudice to the issuance of Notice of Suspension, when warranted;
Submit immediately the following documents for Audit Team’s evaluation, without prejudice to the issuance of Notice of Suspension, when warranted:
Valid justification on the lack of APMV relative to the LGU’s acquisition of motor vehicle using the LGSF-COVID-19 FA to LGUs; and
Duly acknowledged RDS relative to the goods procured by LGU using the LGSF-COVID-19 FA to LGUs and distributed to its constituents
Prospectively, for specific grant/financial assistance received by the LGU and recorded in its Trust Fund books, ensure the reconciliation at a given point in time of the actual unutilized funds against its corresponding SL balance in the books; and
Determine the propriety of the amount to be reverted immediately to the National Treasury in connection with LGSF-COVID-19 FA to LGUs after considering the recommendations cited above.

We recommended that Management ensure that Cashbooks maintained by the AOs conform strictly with the prescribed format and instructions provided in the LTOM, such that the required columns are appropriately and completely filled-up. Likewise, ensure that the required AO’s certification at the end of each month in the Collector’s Cashbooks are complied with.

We recommended that Management designate a Disbursing Officer who shall exclusively perform disbursing functions pursuant to Section 50 of GAAM, Volume III. The designated employee should not act as collector at the same time to eliminate opportunities to conceal errors and irregularities.

We recommended that Management:
Identify each cash advances as to the type of account where the same should be appropriately recorded. Consequently, effect the necessary adjusting entries to correct the balances of affected accounts;
Coordinate with Municipal Treasurer’s Office regarding the maintenance of Cashbook-Cash Advance corresponding to each advances accounts per fund to facilitate its reconciliation with subsidiary accounting records; and
Ensure to uphold reliability of account balances by proper recording of transactions to appropriate accounts in accordance with Annex B of COA Circular No. 2015-009.

We recommended that Management:
Follow-up for immediate submission of the required liquidation documents;
Require all transfer of funds to IA for implementation of projects and programs be covered by a MOA and other documentary requirements as enumerated in Section 3.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 to establish proper accountability and monitoring of the proper utilization of the funds; and
Prospectively, fund transfers must be supported with certification by the Municipal Accountant that funds previously transferred to the IA has been liquidated and accounted for in the books.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the Management:
Coordinate with the Source Agencies on the proper disposition of unspent balances of completed projects;
Effect necessary adjusting entries on the transfer of completed projects to its appropriate accounts; and
Prospectively, ensure that necessary documentations of fund transfers are well-kept, especially for fund transfers, to immediately determine proper disposition of said funds.

We recommended that Management direct the concerned offices to hasten the implementation of the partially and unimplemented PPAs funded by the current and prior years’ continuing appropriations to fully attain the desired socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes essential to the promotion of the general welfare of its constituents.

We recommended that Management review and reconcile the amounts and entries made in the Trust Liabilities-DRRMF account and the details disclosed in the Notes to FS as required under COA Circular No. 2012-002 to report accurately the transactions and information for the LDRRMF.

We recommended that Management require the conduct of physical inventory of the breeding stocks, prepare the required inventory report and reconcile the accounting records and effect necessary adjustments in the books, if any.
We recommended that Management strengthen accounting controls and review the negative or abnormal balances of the Due to GSIS account for proper adjustment in the books of account.
We recommended that Management strictly comply with the provisions of the R-IRR of R.A. No. 9184 in the procurement of goods, particularly on the prohibition of reference to brand names.

We recommended that Management:
Ensure a gender issues/gaps responsive projects/activities identified for funding supported by sex disaggregated data for the attainment of gender equality and women’s empowerment; and
Refrain from incurrence of expenses that cannot be charged to the GAD Budget, to avoid disallowance in the future.

We recommended that Management ensure that RAAOs per function/program/project, allotment class, current/continuing appropriations and fund should be prepared and maintained by the LGU for the timely monitoring of appropriations, allotments, and obligations, ultimately, preventing the incurrence of obligations incurred in excess of allotments received or approved appropriations.

We recommended that Management, through the Accountant, submit to the Audit Team together with the duplicate copies of Bank Statements, DVs, Checks and other documents that would prove the actual transfer to avoid being held liable for the funds which cannot now be traced.
We further recommended that after the transfer is properly proven, direct the OIC-Accountant and the OIC-Treasurer to process the return of the balance to the Trust Fund.  They can use the LBP Account No. ending with xxx-46-36 as the depositary bank account due to its minimal amount.
Lastly, we recommended that Management inquire on the source of the funds and the specific purpose for which it was created, and evaluate the proper action regarding the disposition thereof and submit a report to the Audit Team.

We recommended that Management direct the OIC-Accountant to hasten the preparation and submission of the lacking BRS for review and verification, and henceforth, observe the prescribed period in the submission to the Audit Team pursuant to COA Circular No. 96-011.

We recommended and Management agreed that the City Accountant determine the cause of the negative balances and prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries in order to correct the negative balances of the PPE items, if warranted.

We recommended that the Management direct the City Accountant to compute and to record the depreciation in the books of accounts in order to present fairly the total value of the PPE and Government Equity accounts in the financial statements.  The following entry is proposed:
Dr     Depreciation Expense                                  xxx
Cr Accumulated Depreciation                                  xxx
Henceforth, we recommended that depreciation be recorded monthly and not on a lump sum basis at year end.

We recommended that Management verify the actual physical status of the Infrastructure Projects still recorded in the Construction in Progress Accounts and direct the City Accountant to prepare appropriate entries to reclassify all completed projects to its appropriate property, plant and equipment accounts in accordance with pertinent rules and regulations.
We further recommended that Management direct the OIC-City Accountant to record the following proposed adjusting journal entries for the three completed projects:
Infrastructure Assets					 5,156,500.00
Building and Other Structures 				22,020,767.25
CIP - Infrastructure Assets					 5,156,500.00
CIP - Buildings and Other Structures				22,020,767.25
#
Depreciation- Infrastructure Assets			  260,695.00
Depreciation- Building & Other Structures		1,981,869.05
Prior Period Adjustments 				 5,629,248.06
Accumulated Depreciation – Infrastructure Assets	3,577,762.50
Accumulated Depreciation – Building & Other Structures 	4,294,049.61

We recommended and Management agreed to direct the OIC-City Accountant to record the costs of major repairs incurred as addition to the asset accounts and the related depreciation expenses through the following proposed adjusting journal entries:
Infrastructure Assets					16,679,116.02
Building and Other Structures 				 1,649,200.00
