We likewise recommended and Management agreed to direct the OIC-Accountant to stop the practice of recording the cost of major repair of existing PPE as repair and maintenance.

We recommended and Management agreed to direct the City GSO to prepare and submit to the Audit Team an updated inventory report on the physical count of properties not later than January 31 of each year pursuant to Section 124, Volume I of the NGAS Manual.

We recommended that the OIC-City Accountant reconcile the records, retrieve relevant documents and coordinate with the City Treasurer for the return of the excess funds from completed programs/projects. We also recommended that Management, for financial assistance which remained unutilized, secure authority from the Source Agency to use these for other priority projects of the City.
We further recommended that Management direct the OIC-City Accountant to record the following proposed Journal Entry for the liquidation on the utilized amount of the fund transfer from DILG:
Due to NGAs						2,394,997.00
Prior Period Adjustments				2,394,997.00

We recommended that Management direct the City Accountant to carefully analyze and identify the cause/s of the abnormal balances of the identified accounts and effect the necessary adjustments to establish the correct balances in the subsidiary ledgers.

We recommended and Management agreed to direct the OIC-Accountant to derecognize the Land account by following the proposed adjusting journal entry:
Due from LGUs – Brgy. Jaclupan		₱3,600,000.00
Land  						₱3,600,000.00
We likewise recommended and Management agreed to monitor the implementation of the assistance, such as the transfer of certificate of title and the eventual construction of the Multi-Purpose Building to ensure that the said lot shall be used in accordance with its intended purpose.

We recommended that Management comply with the posting requirements of R.A. 9184 in order to achieve the aims of the law by creating and maintaining its own website where the related procurement documents will be posted aside that of the PhilGEPS website.

We recommended that Management submit a written and acceptable explanation/justification evidenced by applicable laws and regulations on the issue of resorting to Negotiated Procurement for the rental of dump truck for more than 15 days instead of public bidding, to an unqualified supplier/contractor and without authority from the DBM to avoid eventual disallowance of the disbursements.
We also recommended that henceforth, the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) adopt public bidding as the general mode of procurement so that more qualified and eligible bidders can participate and enable the City to obtain the most advantageous contracts in the rental of vehicles.

We recommended that Management submit a written and acceptable justification based on laws, rules and regulations on the deficiencies noted, and henceforth, strictly observe the pertinent provisions of RA 9184 and its 2016 Revised IRR.
We likewise recommended that Management demand from the supplier the amount of ₱1,200,398.06 representing the liquidated damages that should have been deducted from the contract price due to the delayed delivery of the items procured, otherwise, responsible persons should be held liable for the said deficiency.

We recommended that Management:
create its own website and post therein the procurement projects to be undertaken by the City; and
direct the BAC Secretariat in coordination with Public Information Office to come up with additional measures in advertising bid invitations such as posting at the City’s own social media accounts.

We recommended that Management observe prudence in the incurrence of expenditures by giving due consideration of the actual needs and requirements and by always adhering with the economical measures in every procurement activity to be undertaken.

We recommended and Management agreed to submit the lacking documents and a written justification/explanation on the noted deficiencies, for the Audit Team’s verification and evaluation, to avoid disallowance in audit.
Henceforth, we recommended that Management exercise prudence in the disbursement of government funds and ensure that limited government resources are spent on programs/projects/activities with the end view of promoting efficiency, economy and effectiveness in its operation.

We recommended and Management agreed to submit the lacking documents and a written justification/explanation on the noted deficiencies, for the Audit Team’s verification and evaluation, to avoid disallowance in audit. Henceforth, we recommended that Management ensure that any disbursements of public funds are covered with appropriations and the documentary requirements particularly on the purchase of lot are strictly observed.

We recommended that Management inform COA within 10 days after the award of the infrastructure project or before the start of the PPAs that the appropriate project signboards and/or public notices are already posted.

We recommended that Management submit immediately the duly accomplished AAPSI Form for review and validation, and henceforth, observe the prescribed period in the submission to the Audit Team pursuant to General Provisions of the GAA.

We recommended and Management agreed to adhere with the documentary requirements enumerated under Item 5.14 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 and the pertinent rules and regulations in the payment of monetization of accumulated leave credits.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Direct the General Service Office (GSO) Head to submit the Consolidated Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption and Consolidated Monthly Report of Official Travels in CY 2021 together with the Driver’s Trip Tickets to the Audit Team to facilitate the validation of the reasonableness of fuel consumption of the City;
Direct the OIC-Accountant to examine carefully the completeness of the supporting documents for every transaction to ensure that all disbursements are properly documented; and
Direct all Department Heads and Drivers to ensure that Driver’s Trip Tickets are properly accomplished and that all the necessary information indicated in the forms are all filled-out for every trip undertaken.

We recommended that the Management intensify/expedite the hiring or promotion of qualified personnel to enhance its services and attain full implementation of the City’s programs, projects and activities for the optimal benefit of the constituents as well as to provide equal opportunities to the deserving individuals in the community.  Likewise, we recommended that Management adhere to the pertinent Civil Service rules and regulations on the hiring of job order workers.

Nevertheless, we recommended and Management agreed to submit a written and acceptable justification on the five deficiencies noted enough to merit a non-disallowance decision from the audit team.
Henceforth, Management should strictly observe the pertinent provisions of RA 9184 and its 2016 Revised IRR and all other applicable laws, rules and regulations.
We likewise recommended that Management direct the BAC to ensure that all procurements, regardless of modality, are in compliance with the requirements and procedures prescribed in the procurement law especially in avoiding potential conflict of interest.

We recommended and Management agreed to submit the distribution lists duly acknowledged by the actual recipients/beneficiaries so that the distributions of the procured vitamins and relief goods in the total amount of ₱2,545,920.00 could be duly accounted for.

We recommended that Management:
Justify the non-insurance of the properties which now need repair as a result of typhoon Odette and submit convincing explanation why its responsible officials shall not be held liable for the equivalent cost of repairs of these buildings.
Insure all the insurable properties of the City with the GSIS in compliance with the provisions of RA No. 656 and COA Circular No. 2018-002;
Require the GSO to submit to the Audit Team and GSIS the Consolidated PIF not later than April 30 of each year; and
Refund the insurance premium paid totaling ₱52,890.57 for those buildings which were already demolished.

We recommended that Management submit immediately the duly accomplished AAPSI Form for review and validation, and henceforth, observe the prescribed period in the submission to the Audit Team pursuant to General Provisions of the GAA.

We recommended that the City Mayor:
Create an Inventory Committee in consonance with the composition provided under Item 5.2 of COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated              January 31, 2020 and require the Committee members to complete all the preliminary activities and prepare the Physical Inventory Plan before the conduct of inventory taking;
Direct the Inventory Committee to conduct complete physical inventory of all City’s PPE whether acquired through purchase or donation, including those constructed by administration and found at station and undertake all planned activities/procedures in the approved PIP and prepare the RPCPPE pursuant to Item 6.2.10 of COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020; and
Require the General Services Office (GSO) to update its property records based on the result of the physical inventory and reconcile these with the accounting records to come up with the reconciled balances of PPE accounts pursuant to Item 5.12 of COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020.

We reiterated our recommendation that the City Mayor and the City Accountant:
Enforce immediate settlement of all outstanding cash advances totaling ₱3,207,524.25, especially those intended for special purposes pursuant to Item 4.4.2 of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997;
Send   demand   letters to officials and employees who have long outstanding cash advances to settle the overdue amounts within a specific period; and
Otherwise, effect the withholding of their salaries pursuant to              Item 5.1.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002 until such time that the cash advances are fully settled.

We recommended that the City Accountant/City Treasurer:
Immediately return to the Source Agency the excess balance of completed BuB projects or request the approval of the Source Agency to transfer to the General Fund the unexpended balances of completed BuB projects in adherence to COA Circular Nos. 94-013 and 2012-001 dated December 13, 1994 and June 14, 2012; and
Transfer from Trust Fund to the General Fund of the City the unutilized counterpart of completed/unimplemented BuB projects.

We recommended that the City Accountant - Designate return the total amount of ₱982,217.56 to the Bureau of Treasury in compliance with COA-DBM Joint Circular No. 9-81, COA Circular No. 94-013, Executive Order No. 431 and the Supreme Court ruling issued on November 19, 2013.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Health Officer to secure necessary guidelines or basis on the continued grant of AHDP for the period December 20, 2020 to April 30, 2021, otherwise require refunds from recipients.

We recommended that the City of Talisay submit specific law, other statutory authority, or rules and regulations of competent authority expressly authorizing such payment of additional benefit to its officials and employees pursuant to COA Circular   No. 2013-003 dated January 30, 2013. Otherwise, such payment could be considered wanting of legal basis.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the BAC to:
Discontinue the awarding of contract for the procurement of goods and catering services to Talisay City Local Government Employees Multipurpose Cooperative by reason of conflict of interest among BAC members and the officials of said cooperative;
Evaluate carefully the qualifications of participating bidders especially the participation of a cooperative in the City’s procurement opportunities; and
Disqualify bidders/suppliers from participating in the City’s procurement activities when such bidder or participating cooperative is related by consanguinity or affinity within the third civil degree to the Head of the Procuring Entity (HOPE), the BAC members, the Technical Working Group, the BAC Secretariat, Head of the Project Management Office (PMO), or the end-user unit and the consultants in compliance with Sec. 47.2 of RA 9184 and Item 5.1 of GPPB Circular No. 08-2017.

We recommended that the City prepare and submit a one-time report for all in-kind donations to the NDRRMC, through the OCD, copy furnished to the respective COA Auditor, within 10 working days after the end of the quarantine or within 10 working days after the end of each quarter, pursuant to Item II.1.a.2 and II.1.b.1 of COA Circular No. 2020-009 dated April 21, 2020.

We recommended that:
The CPDC and City Engineer and all other implementing offices of the City expedite the implementation of all projects funded by the 20% Development Fund to ensure optimum utilization of the fund so that the services and benefits from these projects are delivered to the constituents without unnecessary delay; and
The CPDC conduct regular monitoring of project implementation of all projects funded by the 20% DF so the desired level of efficiency is achieved.

We recommended that the City Health Officer track the expiration dates/beyond use dates (BUD) of the COVID-19 vaccines donated to the City and prioritize the utilization chronologically from the earliest to the latest.
We also recommended that the CHO coordinate with the Provincial Health Office as to the proper disposal of the expired vaccines.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterate our previous recommendations for Management to:

Prepare and submit copy of the RPCPPE to the Auditor in prescribed format, not later than January 31 of each year. In the preparation of the report, the different column should be properly filled out to ensure that all items are properly identified and accounted for; and



Perform periodic reconciliation of property cards to ensure that all discrepancies are immediately investigated, cleared and reconciled with the books of accounts.
observations.reason:
Partially Implemented | The RPCPPE was not submitted on time as required under Section 124 of the MNGAS for Local Government Units (LGUs). It was submitted only upon demand of the Audit Team.


Comparison of the PPE balances (excluding Construction in Progress) in the RPCPPE and the recorded PPE in the books as of December 31, 2021 disclosed a net variance of ₱67,117,642.85. | Related finding incorporated in Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the Management to:

Re-examine the cash accounts to determine the reasons for the variances and reconcile the individual balances of the Cash in Treasury and Cash in Bank accounts in the GL and Cashbooks/Passbooks, respectively and make the necessary corrections and/or adjustments thereon, if needed, and furnish the Audit Team copy of the Journal Entry Vouchers (JEVs) for verification and audit purposes; and

Prospectively, require both the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to reconcile their records at least quarterly, as required by existing regulations.
observations.reason:
Partially Implemented | Reiterated the observation during Calendar Year (CY) 2021, wherein the cashbook balance and subsidiary ledger balance for Cash in Local Treasury account has a net unreconciled balance of ₱5,919.37 as of November 10, 2021.


Delayed reconciliation. | Related finding incorporated in Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the Management to:

Ensure that the necessary adjusting entries are effected in the books to recognize the RPT and SET receivables for CY 2020; 

Guarantee that the MTO timely furnish the Municipal Accounting Office (MAccO) of the duly certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year; and

Warrant that the MAccO promptly establish the RPT and SET receivables upon receipt from the MTO of the required list.

We recommended the Management to:

Prepare a reconciliation report of reciprocal accounts and take up the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts to ensure that all reconciling items are resolved and/or adjusted on a timely basis; and

Perform constant review of recorded transactions to deter error and omissions.
observations.managementAction: The Management agreed to implement the recommendation.
observations.reason: No enough trained and qualified regular staff.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the Management to:

Coordinate with the Source Agencies on the proper disposition of unspent balances of completed projects;

Effect necessary adjusting entries on the transfer of completed projects; and
	

Prospectively, ensure that necessary documentations of fund transfers are well-kept, especially for fund transfers, to immediately determine proper disposition of said funds.
observations.managementAction: Only those completed projects with supporting documents as to its completion were adjusted/transferred.
observations.reason: No enough trained and qualified regular staff.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the Management to:

Perform periodic verification, analysis and validation of the Due to LGUs account with concerned LGUs; and

Draw necessary journal entries to reflect the correct/adjusted Due to LGUs account balances.
observations.reason: No enough trained and qualified regular staff.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Management to monitor and reconcile the Due to GSIS and Due to Pag-IBIG accounts to determine the cause of the abnormal balances and effect necessary adjustments.
observations.reason: The Due to GSIS account as of December 31, 2021 reflected still an abnormal balance of ₱34,261.35. | Related finding incorporated in Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation:
We commended the Management for its efforts in addressing the perennial recommendation on the preparation of Bank Reconciliation Statements. On the other hand, we recommend that Management take the following courses of action:

Replace with new checks the stale checks that are in the hands of the payee, upon compliance with the required procedures; and

Install a monitoring mechanism that will facilitate notification to payees or holder of checks issued one month prior to the date when issued checks will become stale so that concerned payees will be aware of the depository bank’s action on these issued checks six months after the date of its issuance.

observations.managementAction:
The Management was unable to prioritize the recommendation.



The Management agreed to implement the recommendation.
observations.reason:
During CY 2021, relative to the conduct of cash examination on the cash and accounts of Disbursing Officer (DO), various instances of additional CAs drawn despite non-liquidation of the previous CAs were observed.

The observed instances of additional CAs drawn despite non-liquidation of the previous CAs will result to undue accumulation of outstanding CAs.

There were still provisions in the Circulars not strictly complied by the Management.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:

Identify each cash advances as to the type of account where the same should be appropriately recorded. Consequently, effect the necessary adjusting entries to correct the balances of affected accounts;

Coordinate with Municipal Treasurer’s Office regarding the maintenance of Cashbook-Cash Advance corresponding to each advances accounts per fund to facilitate its reconciliation with subsidiary accounting records; and

Ensure to uphold reliability of account balances by proper recording of transactions to appropriate accounts in accordance with Annex B of COA Circular No. 2015-009.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant agreed and committed to implement the recommendations such that they will record the cash advances to appropriate accounts, indicate proper account titles in accordance with the above-mentioned circular and coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer’s Office to facilitate reconciliation of the accounts.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:

Follow-up for immediate submission of the required liquidation documents;

Require all transfer of funds to IA for implementation of projects and programs be covered by a MOA and other documentary requirements as enumerated in Section 3.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 to establish proper accountability and monitoring of the proper utilization of the funds; and

Prospectively, fund transfers must be supported with certification by the Municipal Accountant that funds previously transferred to the IA has been liquidated and accounted for in the books.
observations.managementAction:
In its Reply, the Management remarked that communication through a follow-up letter to the concerned Barangays was already made. Further, the Management informed that liquidations for the following amounts are at hand for necessary recognition in the books:

Moreover, the Management stressed that no fund transfers were allowed since CY 2020 to 2021 due to the inability of the concerned Barangays to liquidate.
observations.reason: The Management was unable to prioritize the recommendation.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the Management:

Coordinate with the Source Agencies on the proper disposition of unspent balances of completed projects;

Effect necessary adjusting entries on the transfer of completed projects to its appropriate accounts; and

Prospectively, ensure that necessary documentations of fund transfers are well-kept, especially for fund transfers, to immediately determine proper disposition of said funds.
observations.managementAction:
The Management, in its Reply, informed the Audit Team that prior years’ records relative to the above-mentioned projects were revisited and re-evaluated. As a result, the Management arrived at the following:

A total of ₱8,867,850.66 or 28.50 per cent  Development Fund (DF) appropriated to finance key development PPAs remain unutilized as of December 31, 2021, thereby defeating the purpose of maximizing its utilization to achieve the desirable socio-economic development and environmental outcomes of the Municipality.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management direct the concerned offices to hasten the implementation of the partially and unimplemented PPAs funded by the current and prior years’ continuing appropriations to fully attain the desired socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes essential to the promotion of the general welfare of its constituents.
observations.managementAction: The Management agreed to adhere with the audit recommendation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management review and reconcile the amounts and entries made in the Trust Liabilities-DRRMF account and the details disclosed in the Notes to FS as required under COA Circular No. 2012-002 to report accurately the transactions and information for the LDRRMF.
observations.managementAction: The Management acknowledged the need to reconcile the balance of the Trust Liabilities-DRRMF account and the details regarding LDRRMF disclosed in the Notes to FS. In view of this, the Management analyzed the discrepancies noted and arrived at the following:

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require the conduct of physical inventory of the breeding stocks, prepare the required inventory report and reconcile the accounting records and effect necessary adjustments in the books, if any.
observations.managementAction:
The Management agreed to implement the recommendation. In view of this, the Management informed that the Municipal Agriculture Office has already conducted a physical inventory of the breeding stocks as shown below:

Relative to this Inventory Report, the Municipal Accounting Office committed to effect the needed adjustments in the books regarding Breeding Stocks account as soon as the requirements necessitating such adjustments are complied with under existing accounting rules and regulations.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management strengthen accounting controls and review the negative or abnormal balances of the Due to GSIS account for proper adjustment in the books of account.
observations.managementAction: The Management agreed to adhere with the audit recommendation. Further, the Management remarked that since January 2022 the reconciliation of payroll deductions against remittances to GSIS was being done in order to immediately identify and adjust the discrepancies.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management strictly comply with the provisions of the R-IRR of R.A. No. 9184 in the procurement of goods, particularly on the prohibition of reference to brand names.
observations.managementAction: The Management agreed and promised to comply with the audit recommendation.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:

Ensure a gender issues/gaps responsive projects/activities identified for funding supported by sex disaggregated data for the attainment of gender equality and women’s empowerment; and

Refrain from incurrence of expenses that cannot be charged to the GAD Budget, to avoid disallowance in the future.

observations.managementAction: During the Exit Conference, the BAC remarked that the Head of the BAC Secretariat only assumed such designation on November 2021. The Head of the BAC Secretariat acknowledged the deficiencies observed and committed on exerting its best efforts in order to ensure the LGU’s compliance with the Audit Team’s recommendations. Moreover, the heads of other LGU Offices such as MEO, MDRRMO, and others assured its cooperation with the BAC, thru its BAC Secretariat, on the timely submission of relevant documents originating from their respective offices as necessary attachments to the perfected contracts to be submitted for auditorial and technical review.
observations.reason:
During CY 2021, the Municipality was unable to submit 26 perfected contracts for auditorial and technical review. 

Moreover, delay and incomplete documentary requirements were noted on the eight perfected contracts submitted for review during CY 2021. | Related finding incorporated in Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management ensure that RAAOs per function/program/project, allotment class, current/continuing appropriations and fund should be prepared and maintained by the LGU for the timely monitoring of appropriations, allotments, and obligations, ultimately, preventing the incurrence of obligations incurred in excess of allotments received or approved appropriations.
observations.managementAction: In its Reply, the Management assured that, despite limited manpower, the MAccO will prepare and maintain the RAAOs for CY 2022.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the Management to:

Fully implement the six unfinished developmental projects targeted under the 20 per cent DF for CY 2020;

Reprogram the unexpended balance of Corona Virus Disease 2019 (COVID-19) related Programs/Projects/Activities (PPAs) as of December 31, 2020 to allowable PPAs under the 20 per cent DF of the ensuing year; and

Direct the Municipal Development Council (MDC) to ensure that the development projects identified are feasible and can be implemented within the year with the proposed budget allocation.
observations.managementAction: The Management agreed to implement the recommendation.
observations.reason: A total of ₱8,867,850.66 or 28.50 per cent DF appropriated to finance key development PPAs remain unutilized as of December 31, 2021. | Related finding incorporated in Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Management to select/consider strictly PPAs that would be charged to the 20 per cent DF in accordance with the aforementioned rules and regulations. Likewise, financial transactions be scrutinized carefully to ensure that only items that are directly associated in the implementation of the targeted PPAs are properly charged to the Fund to maximize its utilization for more beneficial purpose.

We recommended the Management to warrant that the LDRRMF’s utilization were limited to the eligible projects and activities, in accordance with the COA Circular No. 2012-002 and NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-1, likewise, guarantee the propriety of the disbursements charged against such eligible projects and activities, without prejudice to the issuance of Notice of Suspension (NS)/Notice of Disallowance (ND), when warranted.

We recommended the Management to:

Ensure that only eligible projects and activities, in accordance with the NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-1, are appropriated under the current year’s five per cent LDRRMF and the unexpended LDRRMF; and 

Warrant the propriety of the disbursements charged against the projects and activities programmed under the LDRRMF, in accordance with the above-cited existing rules and regulations, without prejudice to the issuance of NS/ND.

We recommended the Municipal Accountant to ensure adequate disclosure of information in the Notes to FS as required under COA Circular No. 2012-02 to enhance the relevance and understandability of the FS to the users of the same.
observations.reason: During CY 2021, the amounts and details of the LDRRMF was presented in the Notes to FS. However, reported balance of Special Trust Fund under the Trust Liabilities-DRRMF account as of December 31, 2021 amounting to ₱2,485,120.81 could not be ascertained due to discrepancies noted in the presented balance of the account in the DSFPos and Notes to FS. | Related finding incorporated in Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the Management to:

Ensure coordination of the concerned units (Municipal Accounting Office, Municipal Budget Office and Municipal Treasurer’s Office) to facilitate the timely transfer of the unexpended LDRRMF from the General Fund (GF) books to the TF books through the prescribed accounting entries; and

Effect the necessary correcting entries for the erroneous debit to Due to Other Funds related to the transfers made on the previous years.

We recommended the Management to:

Consider the inclusion of projects and activities to be funded from the unexpended LDRRMF already transferred in the TF books, in the preparation of the current year’s LDRRMFIP; and

Suppose the unexpended LDRRMF was not initially programmed in the current year’s LDRRMFIP, ensure that any utilization thereto, during the year for projects and activities to help support the DRRM activities of the LDRRMC within the next five years from the year of transfer, the LDRRMFIP shall accordingly be revised and updated to reflect the same.

We recommended the Local School Board to follow strictly JC No. 1, s. 2017 of the DepEd, DBM and DILG in the budget preparation of the SEF to ensure appropriate identification of PPAs and budget allocation.

We recommended the Management to:

Strictly follow the provisions of R.A. No. 9184; and


Stop the practice of resorting to splitting requisitions/contracts.
observations.reason:
Not all provisions of R.A. No. 9184 were still not strictly followed.

During CY 2021, purchase requisitions for the procurement of hardware materials totaling ₱115,400.00 were splitted. | A related Audit Observation Memorandum (AOM) was issued.

A related AOM was issued.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the Management to:

Ensure that, in case of specific fund transfers/grants received (such as the Bayanihan Grant to Cities/Municipalities or BGCM), the specific guidelines issued corresponding to its utilization (such as LBC No. 125) be faithfully complied and adhered with;

Require the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to strictly comply with existing laws, rules and regulations on procurement; and

Ensure that transactions are paid only when supported by complete documentation, without prejudice to the issuance of NS/ND, when warranted.
observations.reason: Not all provisions of R.A. No. 9184 were still not strictly followed. | The Management agreed to implement the recommendation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Management to ensure that, in case of specific fund transfers/grants received (such as the BGCM), the specific guidelines issued corresponding to its utilization (such as LBC No. 125) be faithfully complied and adhered with, thus, securing the achievement of its purpose and the intent of the law where the same emanated without prejudice to the issuance of NS/ND, when warranted.

We reiterated our previous recommendations for the Management to:

Give preferential attention on ensuring that parcels of land declared as owned by the Municipality are covered by Torrens Title/ Transfer Certificate of Title (TCT) duly issued by the Register of Deeds.
observations.managementAction: Some parcels of land declared as owned by the Municipality are still not covered by Torrens Title/TCT duly issued by the Register of Deeds due to some lacking documents needed for titling. | Management agreed to comply with the recommendation.

observations.recommendation:
We commended the Management for its efforts in addressing the perennial recommendation on the preparation of Bank Reconciliation Statements (BRS). On the other hand, we recommended the Management to: 

In coordination with the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant, determine the appropriate disposition on these transactions such as (a) in case the stale checks are still in the possession of the local treasury, adjustments in the books of the affected accounts or replacement of checks; and (b) in case the stale checks are in the hands of the payee, replacement of new checks upon compliance with the required procedures; and

Install a monitoring mechanism that will facilitate notification to payees or holder of checks issued one month prior to the date when issued checks will become stale so that concerned payees will be aware of the depository bank’s action on these issued checks six months after the date of its issuance.
observations.managementAction: The Management was unable to prioritize the recommendation. | Management agreed to comply with the recommendation.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the Management to:

Upon receipt of the liquidation documents, require a representative from the Office of the Municipal Engineer to conduct inspection and validate the reported completed projects.
observations.managementAction: The Management was unable to prioritize the recommendation. | Management agreed to comply with the recommendation.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our previous recommendations for the Management to require the BAC and other departments concerned to: 

Promptly submit requests for contract review and technical inspection for completed projects to the COA to avoid unnecessary delays in the conduct of contract evaluation and technical inspection; and 

Observe the necessity of compliance on the required documentary requirements for submission within the prescribed timeframe for review and inspection.
observations.reason:

Related finding incorporated in Part II of this report. The BAC is exerting efforts to settle the unnecessary delays on the submission of request for contract review and technical inspection for those completed projects.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the Management to:

Optimize the utilization of the 20 per cent LDF and implement the programs, activities and projects (PAPs) within the timelines in order to achieve the socio-economic and environmental outcomes, essential in the attainment of its goals and objectives of a desired quality of life for its constituents; and

Direct the MDC to ensure that the development projects identified are feasible and can be implemented within the year with the proposed budget allocation.
observations.reason:
A total of ₱8,867,850.66 or 28.50 per cent DF appropriated to finance key development PPAs remained unutilized as of December 31, 2021.

As of December 31, 2020, there were still projects not fully implemented. | Related finding incorporated in Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Management to ensure that only expenditures that have direct bearing or falling within the concept of disaster preparedness, prevention and mitigation activities are included in the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management (LDRRM) plan and charged to the Fund. We likewise recommended the replenishment of the amount paid for loan amortization charged to the LDRRMF to support disaster risk management activities.

We recommended the Management to:

Enhance monitoring of LDRRMF utilization focusing on PPAs requiring the highest priority in terms of urgency and/or impact on the four thematic areas of DRRM; and

Ensure efficient and effective implementation of all programmed projects identified during the budget year to optimize the utilization of the fund and to warrant that goals and objectives of reducing disaster risk and enhancing disaster preparedness and response capabilities are achieved.
observations.reason:
There were still not fully implemented PPAs in the current year’s five per cent LDRRMF.

During CY 2021, the unutilized current year’s five per cent LDRRMF amounted to
₱709,176.89, or 13 per cent of ₱5,285,879.55. | Management agreed to comply with the recommendation.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our previous recommendations for the Management to:

Direct the Accountant to immediately submit the unsubmitted CY 2019 Monthly Trial Balances and Quarterly FS and take appropriate immediate remedial measures such as augmenting the Municipal Accountant’s Office of personnel from other Departments, especially during report preparations and/or allowing them to render overtime, subject to the availability of funds, in order to comply with the timely preparation and submission of these mandatory financial reports, pursuant to mandated deadlines. Henceforth, to prepare and submit the same, with complete Schedules and Annexes within the prescribed timeframe in accordance with the above-mentioned existing rules and regulations.

We recommended the Management to: 

Observe prompt submission of DVs and ORs including the corresponding reports each month on or before the 10th day of the ensuing month; and

Strict adherence to the cited rules and regulations on the rendition of accounts and reports be enforced by the concerned officials in order not to incur penalty as provided in Section 122(2) of P.D. No. 1445.

We recommended the Management to:

Take appropriate immediate remedial measures, such as augmenting the Accountant’s Office of personnel by hiring additional qualified personnel to specifically prepare the monthly BRS and/or allowing the personnel in-charge of said BRS to render overtime, subject to availability of funds in order to submit the same to the Auditor on the mandated deadline.

We recommended that Management take the following courses of action:

Necessary adjustments, if any, should be affected to avoid accumulation thereof and to present the correct balance of the cash in bank accounts in the financial statements.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that the City Mayor:
Require the City Inventory Committee to complete the conduct of physical count of all City’s PPE so they could submit the complete Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) not later than January 31 of each year pursuant to Section 124 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I;
Require the City Accountant and the GSO to implement regular reconciliation of their records and undertake specific measures to eliminate the discrepancies between the two records; and
Require the City Accountant and the GSO to maintain updated PPE ledger and property cards, respectively so reconciliation of the two records could be done easily.

We recommended that the City Mayor obtain authority from the Sangguniang Panlungsod thru an SP Resolution approving the transfer without cost or donation of the firearms and air conditioning units to the PNP so that the accountability and monitoring of these properties are no longer the responsibility of the City.
We further recommended that the City Accountant ascertain that the properties transferred are recorded in the PNP books of accounts.

We reiterated our recommendation that the City Mayor as Chairman of the Local School Board to:
Refrain from utilizing the Special Education Fund for trainings, seminars and other school activities since they are not allowable expenses to be charged against the Fund pursuant to Item 4.0 of DepEd, DBM and DILG Joint Circular (JC) No. 1, series of 2017 dated January 17, 2017; and
Allocate and utilize the SEF on the priority programs and projects provided under Item 4.0 of the above mentioned Joint Circular.

We recommended that the City Accountant reverse the erroneous entry to correct the balances of Loans Receivable and Loans Payable accounts to reflect a fair and reliable presentation of account balances in the Financial Statement of the City as of year-end.

We recommended that:
The City Treasurer cancel all unclaimed checks which have become stale and present the cancelled checks in the Report of Checks Issued (RCI) after the last check issued and submit the original DVs and supporting documents to the City Accountant for the preparation of Journal Entry Voucher (JEV);
Further, issue written notice to payees of checks at least one month before the checks become stale and immediately inform the City Accountant of any check cancellation to avoid the accumulation of outstanding checks in compliance with the requirement of Section 59 of the MNGAS for LGUs; and
The City Accountant review outstanding checks in the Bank Reconciliation Statement as of year-end and determine checks not negotiated for more than six months or stale checks and coordinate with the City Treasurer for cancellation of stale checks.
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the BAC to require the suppliers to submit an Omnibus Sworn Statement pursuant to the requirement of RA No. 9184 and its RIRR and the GPPB.

We recommended that the OIC-City Treasurer instruct the Special Disbursing Officer to:
Check the rental payments made to the owners/drivers of vehicles rented covering the periods where overlapping of dates occurred and determine if there were overpayments made;
Require the SDO/owners to refund to the City in case double/overpayments on rentals of vehicles occurred; and
Thoroughly verify/scrutinize supporting documents to be attached to the Liquidation Report as settlement of cash advances for succeeding cash advance.
We also recommended that the City adhere to RA No. 9184 or the GPPB guidelines on Emergency Procurement in the rental of vehicles and follow the existing regulation that all payments must be paid by check except only on instances when it is impractical or impossible to make payments by check pursuant to COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to update its Annual Procurement Plan (APP), prepare the Procurement Management Reports (PMR), post to the City’s official website and submit to the Government Procurement Policy Board (GPPB) as provided under GPPB Circular No. 02-2020 dated May 20, 2020.

We recommended that the City Mayor and the City Development Council together with the City Planning Development Office and the City Engineer’s Office should adhere to Item 2.3 and 3.3 of JMC No. 2017-1 of DBM and DILG dated               February 22, 2017 in the implementation of projects funded under the 20% DF and ensure that these projects contribute to the attainment of desirable socio-economic development.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City DRRM Officer in coordination of the CDRRM Council to formulate and implement disaster prevention and mitigation preparedness plans and projects as well as to maximize the use of its available LDRRM Funds so that the City will be prepared in terms of facilities and the communities will have the capacity to respond in case of calamity.

We recommended that the Accountant-Designate immediately transfer the unutilized balances of the DRRMF-MOOE for CY 2020 to the STF under the Trust Fund books pursuant to Item 5.1.10 of COA Circular No. 2012-12 dated         September 12, 2012 and strictly adhere to the foregoing regulation regarding transfer of unexpended QRF and DRRMF-MOOE.

Based on the foregoing observations, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor
require the:

a. Municipal Accountant and OIC – Municipal General Services Officer to give
priority to the reconciliation of the Municipality’s records and to effect the
necessary adjustments in the books or Inventory Reports for the noted
discrepancies;

b. Inventory Committee to facilitate the immediate disposal of those unserviceable
properties in order not to expose those properties to elements that cause further
deterioration or loss of salvaged value and to enable the municipality to
efficiently use the space occupied by the unserviceable properties. Likewise,
instruct the Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjusting entries in
the book of accounts upon disposal of the said property; and

c. Municipal Accountant to review all the items included in the PPE accounts and
reclassify the identified misclassified items totaling P1,711,940.00 to their
proper PPE accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Budget Officer and
the Municipal Accountant to:

a. refrain from utilizing the current year’s appropriation to pay prior year’s
unrecorded obligation and to strictly adhere with existing laws, rules and
regulations governing the expenditures within the limits of the current
appropriations otherwise, transactions of the same nature in the future will be
disallowed in audit;

b. ensure that the funds are exclusively used for the purpose for which they were
appropriated; and

c. see to it that all valid/lawful obligations are properly taken up in the accounts of
the year they were incurred, pursuant to Section 350 of RA No. 7160 and
Sections 4(1) and 119 of PD No. 1445.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:

a) Municipal Accountant in coordination with Municipal Human Resources Office to:
 exert extra effort to locate the supporting documents pertaining to
unpaid/underpayment of GSIS premium of municipal officials and employees
as per schedule provided by GSIS;
 compare the documents/records with that of the GSIS schedule to determine if
really there are unpaid/underpayments of premiums for remittance to GSIS;
 Henceforth, review the GSIS monthly deductions and ensure that the timely
remittance of monthly premiums, loans and contributions are accurate/exact
amount of contributions withheld; and
 strictly abide with the provisions of RA No. 8291 in order to avoid
administrative sanction as provided in Section 5.c.

b) Municipal Accountant and her staff to:
 expedite the analysis of the Due to GSIS account to eliminate the P120,112.73
negative balance and make necessary adjustment/s, if warranted. Attach
relevant documents and analysis of the accounts to the JEVs for any adjustment
to be made;
 maintain and update subsidiary records for all accounts; and
 carefully check, analyze, review and promptly record the transactions to arrive
at an accurate and correct balances of the accounts in the financial
statements.

We recommended and the management agreed that the Municipal Accountant effect
the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts to reflect the actual insurance
expenses incurred and recognize the balance under the Prepaid Insurance account.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:

a) record subsequent procurement of welfare goods, supplies and materials under
appropriate Inventory account upon purchase, and to recognize the related
expenses only when supported with Report of Supplies and Material Issued and
pre-numbered Requisition and Issue Slip; and

b) submit immediately the duly acknowledged distribution lists for the above stated
disbursements and photos of distribution of welfare goods and/or written
justification/explanation to the Audit Team for the non submission.

We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the GSO and the
Inspector to submit a written justification/explanation on the differences on
specifications of the 1,900 sack of rice as per pictures (Maharlika, 25 kgs) attached
and the delivered/accepted goods (Sinandomeng, 50 kgs).

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Accountable Officers to
refrain from transferring their cash accountability to other personnel.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to:

a) Require the Municipal Engineer to submit an explanation on the extensive delay
incurred or abandonment of the project and the actions taken thereof;

b) Issue a written notice to the contractor for possible termination of the contracts;

c) Facilitate the implementation and completion of the abandoned/unfinished public
market;

d) Submit justification letter on the different names of the referred Lessee and the
signatory in the Lease Agreement and/or documents authorizing the signatory to
sign for and on behalf of the President and CEO of Philippine Primark Properties,
Inc; and

e) Submit the PPP Code of Talisay as per Sangguniang Bayan Ordinance No. 2017-
030 dated April 3, 2017 and the related documents prior to the award of the
contract of lease agreement.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to
ensure that payments of hazard pay are duly supported by an authority to physically
report for work and proof of actual service rendered during the period being claimed.
Likewise, submit immediately the documentary requirements to the Audit Team to
prevent the issuance of Notice of Suspension and/or Disallowance.

We recommended and the management agreed that the Municipal Mayor require the
Human Resource Management Officer, Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant
to control the accommodation given to financing institutions and ensure that
minimum monthly take-home pay of all officials and employees shall not be lower
than Five Thousand Pesos (P5,000.00) pursuant to General Appropriations Act.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Bids and Awards Committee
(BAC) to ensure that all procurement projects of the Municipality regardless of
modality are properly included in the approved APP and all changes thereto are
covered by supplemental/revised APP.

We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Accountant and the BAC to submit immediately the lacking documents (presented
in Annexes J & K) and/or written justification/explanation to the Audit Team for
verification and to address the deficiencies enumerated.

Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that all documentary
requirements for emergency purchase under RA No. 11469 as well as the provision of
Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445 are complied with before certifying as to completeness
of the supporting documents of the disbursement vouchers prior to payment, to avoid
audit sanctions.

We recommended that the Municipal Social Welfare and Development Office
(MSWDO) and Municipal Treasurer Office submit a written
explanation/justification for the following deficiencies noted above:

a. Non-issuance of OR on the receipt of the SAP-ESP from DSWD;
b. Submission of SAP-ESP Liquidation Report to the DSWD beyond the
prescribed period;
c. Non-issuance of OR on the returned SAP-ESP; and
d. Lapses/deficiencies on the supporting documents submitted.

We also recommended that the MSWDO, through coordination with the
corresponding Barangay Officials, submit the lacking documents of the 110 SAP
beneficiaries to support the receipt of subsidy.

Management’s comment/s:
Management’s comment/s or reply for this observation was not yet received by the Audit
Team as of this reporting.

We recommended and the management agreed that the Municipal Mayor instruct
MDRRMO and MSWDO to:

a) Ensure that donations are utilized for the intended purpose in a timely manner;
and

b) Fast track the utilization of the unexpended balance of the cash donations to fund
for the relief and recovery programs by determining the immediate needs of
people affected by Taal volcanic eruption in order to uplift their living conditions.

We recommended and the management agreed that the Municipal Mayor require the
Municipality’s Gender and Development Council to submit regularly the GAD Plan
and Budget as well as its Accomplishment Reports with the corresponding actual
costs to the Audit Team for review and evaluation of budgeted PPAs vis-à-vis its
accomplishments.

We recommended and Management agreed to a thorough revalidation of the listed ineligible beneficiaries.  In addition, should the revalidation prove that the listed persons are indeed ineligible, we henceforth recommended that the Local Chief Executive, the City Accountant and the City Social Welfare Development Officer send demand letters to the 2,629 ineligible beneficiaries for the immediate refund of the illegally received subsidy totaling ₱15,774,000.00.

We recommended that Management require the City Accountant in coordination with the City Treasurer for the immediate refund to the DSWD the amount of ₱846,000.00 representing refunds from unqualified beneficiaries of the SAP.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Disbursing Officer/s to explain in writing why the distribution of the subsidy was made beyond the specified time frame contrary to the guidelines set in DSWD MC No. 09.

We recommended and Management agreed that the CSWDO verify the status of the above beneficiaries and check whether they were disqualified from receiving the second tranche of the cash assistance.
Further, if these beneficiaries were still included in the 2nd tranche, we recommended that the CSWDO facilitate communication for the return of cash assistance received from the LGU.

We recommended and Management agreed that the CSWDO verify if these SAP recipients received the Pantawid grant and check whether they were disqualified from receiving the second tranche of the cash assistance if they had received both Pantawid and SAP subsidy for the month of April 2020.
Further, if these beneficiaries were still included in the 2nd tranche, we recommended that the CSWDO facilitate communication for the return of cash assistance received from LGU.

We recommended that Management require the Barangay Captains to submit the distribution lists duly signed by the actual recipient/beneficiaries so that the distribution of the procured rice in the total amount of ₱134,431,695.20 to its intended beneficiaries could be duly accounted for.

We recommended and Management agreed to submit a justification for undertaking the Emergency Procurement under the Bayanihan Act without complying with the requirements of GPPB Circular No. 01-2020 and to be always updated with the issuances of the GPPB as the governing board for all the procurement activities of the government.

We recommended and Management agreed that the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) comply with the mandatory posting of the updated APP, NOA, and other information relative to the Emergency Procurement under the Bayanihan Act in the GPPB Online Portal, as required in GPPB Circular No. 1-2020.   Henceforth, we recommended that the BAC be updated with the issuances and advisories of the GPPB by visiting their website regularly to avoid any more non-compliance in the future.
We also recommended and Management agreed to submit the required supporting documents to substantiate the validity of the transaction as required under GPPB Circular No. 01-2020 and RA 9184.
We recommended and Management agreed to submit the updated/supplemental LDRRMFIP to support the disbursements for COVID-19 response taken from the LDRRMF pursuant to DBM LBC No. 124.

We recommended and Management agreed to submit the lacking documents as required under GPPB Circular No. 01-2020 dated April 6, 2020. We also recommend that Management require the preparation of the MOA on payments of the same nature to establish proper utilization and monitoring of government funds.
We recommended and Management agreed to submit the lacking required necessary documents to establish the validity of the transactions in pursuance to RA 9184 and COA Circular 2012-001.

We recommended and Management agreed to submit a written explanation on the deficiencies noted and to submit the above-specified necessary documentary requirements pursuant to Section 4(6) of PD 1445.
We recommended and Management agreed to submit the required supporting documents to substantiate the validity of the transaction as required under GPPB Circular No. 01-2020 dated April 6, 2020.
We recommended and Management agreed to submit an explanation or justification on the deficiencies noted for the Audit Team’s evaluation.

We recommended that Management post the monthly report on fund utilization and status of implementation of PPAs in at least three (3) conspicuous public places in the locality as required under Section 4 of DBM LBC No. 125 dated April 7, 2020.
We recommended that Management explain in writing why the donations from the private individuals and entities were unutilized.

We recommended and Management agreed to provide an explanation/justification regarding the breach of enhanced community quarantine protocols by allowing all employees and workers to physically report to work and failing to formulate and implement alternative work arrangements. Also, we recommended to submit written authority for employees and workers who physically reported to work during enhanced community quarantine status to support the payment of hazard pay.

We reiterate our previous recommendations for Management to:
Prepare and submit copy of the RPCPPE to the Auditor in prescribed format, not later than January 31 of each year. In the preparation of the report, the different column should be properly filled out to ensure that all items are properly identified and accounted for; and
Perform periodic reconciliation of property cards to ensure that all discrepancies are immediately investigated, cleared and reconciled with the books of accounts.

We recommended the Management to:
Re-examine the cash accounts to determine the reasons for the variances and reconcile the individual balances of the Cash in Treasury and Cash in Bank accounts in the GL and Cashbooks/Passbooks, respectively and make the necessary corrections and/or adjustments thereon, if needed, and furnish the Audit Team copy of the Journal Entry Vouchers (JEVs) for verification and audit purposes; and
Prospectively, require both the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to reconcile their records at least quarterly, as required by existing regulations.

We recommended the Management to:
Ensure that the necessary adjusting entries are effected in the books to recognize the RPT and SET receivables for CY 2020;
Guarantee that the MTO timely furnish the MAccO of the duly certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year; and
Warrant that the MAccO promptly establish the RPT and SET receivables upon receipt from the MTO of the required list.

We recommended the Management to:
Prepare a reconciliation report of reciprocal accounts and take up the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts to ensure that all reconciling items are resolved and/or adjusted on a timely basis; and
Perform constant review of recorded transactions to deter error and omissions.

We recommended the Management to:
Coordinate with the Source Agencies on the proper disposition of unspent balances of completed projects;
Effect necessary adjusting entries on the transfer of completed projects; and
Prospectively, ensure that necessary documentations of fund transfers are well-kept, especially for fund transfers, to immediately determine proper disposition of said funds.

We recommended the Management to:
Perform periodic verification, analysis and validation of the Due to LGUs account with concerned LGUs; and
Draw necessary journal entries to reflect the correct/adjusted Due to LGUs account balances.
We recommended the Management to monitor and reconcile the Due to GSIS and Due to Pag-IBIG accounts to determine the cause of the abnormal balances and effect necessary adjustments.

We commended the Management for its efforts in addressing the perennial recommendation on the preparation of BRS. On the other hand, we recommended that Management to take the following courses of action:
Replace with new checks the stale checks that are in the hands of the payee, upon compliance with the required procedures; and
Install a monitoring mechanism that will facilitate notification to payees or holder of checks issued one month prior to the date when issued checks will become stale so that concerned payees will be aware of the depository bank’s action on these issued checks six months after the date of its issuance.

We recommended the Management to:
Fully implement the six unfinished developmental projects targeted under the 20 per cent DF for CY 2020;
Reprogram the unexpended balance of COVID-19 related PPAs as of December 31, 2020 to allowable PPAs under the 20 per cent DF of the ensuing year; and
Direct the Municipal Development Council to ensure that the development projects identified are feasible and can be implemented within the year with the proposed budget allocation.

We recommended the Management to select/consider strictly PPAs that would be charged to the 20 per cent DF in accordance with the aforementioned rules and regulations. Likewise, financial transactions be scrutinized carefully to ensure that only items that are directly associated in the implementation of the targeted PPAs are properly charged to the Fund to maximize its utilization for more beneficial purpose.

We recommended the Management to warrant that the LDRRMF’s utilization were limited to the eligible projects and activities, in accordance with the COA Circular No. 2012-002 and NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-1, likewise, guarantee the propriety of the disbursements charged against such eligible projects and activities, without prejudice to the issuance of Notice of Suspension/Notice of Disallowance, when warranted.

We recommended the Management to:
Ensure that only eligible projects and activities, in accordance with the NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-1, are appropriated under the current year’s five per cent LDRRMF and the unexpended LDRRMF; and
Warrant the propriety of the disbursements charged against the projects and activities programmed under the LDRRMF, in accordance with the above-cited existing rules and regulations, without prejudice to the issuance of notice of suspension/notice of disallowance.

We recommended the Municipal Accountant to ensure adequately disclosure of information in the Notes to Financial Statements as required under COA Circular No. 2012-02 to enhance the relevance and understandability of the FS to the users of the same.

We recommended the Management to:
Ensure coordination of the concerned units (Municipal Accounting Office, Municipal Budget Office and Municipal Treasurer’s Office) to facilitate the timely transfer of the unexpended LDRRMF from the GF books to the TF books through the prescribed accounting entries; and
Effect the necessary correcting entries for the erroneous debit to Due to Other Funds related to the transfers made on the previous years.

We recommended the Management to:
Consider the inclusion of projects and activities to be funded from the unexpended LDRRMF already transferred in the TF books, in the preparation of the current year’s LDRRMFIP; and
Suppose the unexpended LDRRMF was not initially programmed in the current year’s LDRRMFIP, ensure that any utilization thereto, during the year for projects and activities to help support the DRRM activities of the LDRRMC within the next five years from the year of transfer, the LDRRMFIP shall accordingly be revised and updated to reflect the same.

We recommended the Local School Board to follow strictly Joint Circular (JC) No. 1, s. 2017 of the DepEd, DBM and DILG in the budget preparation of the SEF to ensure appropriate identification of PPAs and budget allocation.

We recommended the Management to:
Strictly follow the provisions of R.A. No. 9184; and
Stop the practice of resorting to splitting requisitions/contracts.

We recommended the Management to:
Ensure that, in case of specific fund transfers/grants received (such as the BGCM), the specific guidelines issued corresponding to its utilization (such as LBC No. 125) be faithfully complied and adhered with;
Require the BAC to strictly comply with existing laws, rules and regulations on procurement; and
Ensure that transactions are paid only when supported by complete documentation, without prejudice to the issuance of Notice of Suspension/Notice of Disallowance, when warranted.

We recommended the Management to ensure that, in case of specific fund transfers/grants received (such as the BGCM), the specific guidelines issued corresponding to its utilization (such as LBC No. 125) be faithfully complied and adhered with, thus, securing the achievement of its purpose and the intent of the law where the same emanated, without prejudice to the issuance of Notice of Suspension/Notice of Disallowance, when warranted.

We recommended that the City Mayor:
Require the City Inventory Committee to complete the conduct of physical count of all City’s PPE so they could submit the complete Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) not later than January 31 of each year pursuant to Section 124 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I;

Require the City Accountant and the GSO to implement regular reconciliation of their records and undertake specific measures to eliminate the discrepancies between the two records; and

Require the City Accountant and the GSO to maintain updated PPE ledger and property cards, respectively so reconciliation of the two records could be done easily.
