observations.recommendation:
Require the Inventory Committee to conduct a complete physical inventory of all government properties  and  submit  the  Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) not later than January 31 of each year pursuant  to  Section 124  of  the  MNGAS  for  LGUs, Volume  I so the Audit Team has ample time to validate the reported balances of PPE.
Require the Inventory Committee to locate the whereabouts of motorcycles reported as unserviceable in the RPCPPE but were not found at the City Motor Pool.
Require the City Accountant-Designate and the GSO to implement the immediate reconciliation of 	their records inasmuch as the discrepancy affects or involves not only motor vehicles and other transportation equipment but applies also to other PPE accounts.

Direct the GSO to file an application for disposal of all unserviceable transportation equipment with the COA Audit Team following the procedures provided under Section 167, COA Circular No. 92-386 dated October 20, 1992.
Require the City Accountant to drop from the book of accounts the book value of the disposed property pursuant to Section 195 of same COA Circular.

We recommended that the City strictly comply with the requirements of Section 37 of RA No. 9003 to convert open dumpsite into controlled dumpsite.  In addition, the City Mayor directs the City Official to expedite the construction of a sanitary landfill as final disposal of residual wastes and adopt a systematic, comprehensive and ecological wastes management program and encourage waste reduction/reuse/recycling and composting as strategies to reduce the solid wastes.
Moreover, the City include in the Annual Budget as well as in the Annual Investment Plan (AIP) the procurement of a brand new bulldozer or compactor to be stationed at the dumpsite so that a heavy equipment is readily available to push large volume of solid wastes for levelling and compacting wastes discharged from dump trucks.
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Officials to fast track the submission of the requirements in securing the Environmental Compliance Certificate to facilitate the construction of the one-cell Sanitary Landfill to address the problem of the City on waste management.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City officials who are liable for the disallowance to settle the amount of disallowance immediately to the Cashier.  Further, we recommended that the City Mayor direct the Cashier/Treasurer to withhold payment of any money due such persons liable pursuant to Sections 3, Rule XIII of the 2009 Revised Rules and Procedures of the Commission on Audit.

We recommended that the City Development Council (CDC) ensure that projects to be funded by the 20% Development Fund (DF) adhere to the guidelines under Item 3.0 of Joint Circular No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017.
We further recommended that the City Budget Office and the City Accounting Office properly evaluate expenses chargeable against the 20% DF and must discontinue charging expenses to the 20% DF that do not partake of the nature of investment or capital expenditure.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the Accountant-Designate to immediately transfer the unutilized prior years’ balances of the QRF and DRRMF-MOOE to the STF under the Trust Fund books pursuant to Item 5.1.10 of COA Circular No. 2012-12 dated September 12, 2012.
We also recommended that the City Disaster Risk Reduction Council (CDRRMC) promptly decide on the use of the unexpended balance of the LDRRMF which shall be incorporated in the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund and Investment Plan (LDRRMFIP) pursuant to Item 4.4 of COA Circular No. 2012-012 dated September 12, 2012.

We recommended that the City Local Finance Committee composed of the City Planning and Development Coordinator, City Budget Officer and the City Treasurer to properly determine the income reasonably projected as collectible for the ensuing fiscal year, recommend appropriate tax and other revenue measures considered realistic and feasible to support all planned projects, programs and activities included in the Annual Budget pursuant to Section 318 of R.A. No. 7160.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:

a. Municipal Accountant to ensure that all small value assets with useful life of more
than one year but costing less than P10,000.00 are properly classified and
recorded accordingly and to prepare journal entries and effect the necessary
adjustments on the PPE, Accumulated Depreciation and Government Equity
accounts for a fair presentation of the accounts in the financial statements;

b. General Services Officer (GSO) to issue the corresponding Inventory Custodian
Slip (ICS) to all the end-users of the small tangible items and to cancel the
Acknowledgement Receipt for Equipment (ARE) previously issued;

c. GSO to take immediate action to facilitate the appraisal and disposal of the
abovementioned unserviceable properties as required by PD No. 1445 to prevent
exposure to different elements and further deterioration of its values; and

d. creation of a Committee to reconcile the result of the physical count of all PPEs
with the property and accounting records.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Budget Officer and
the Municipal Accountant to:

a. refrain from utilizing the current year’s appropriation to pay prior year’s
unrecorded obligations and to strictly adhere with existing laws, rules and
regulations governing the expenditures within the limits of the current appropriations;

b. ensure that the funds are exclusively used for the purpose for which they were
appropriated; and

c. see to it that all valid/lawful obligations are properly taken up in the accounts of
the year they were incurred, pursuant to Section 350 of RA No. 7160 and Sections
4(1) and 119 of PD No. 1445.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to:

a. stop the practice of giving financial assistance directly to the school/school heads
for the operation and maintenance of school instead, consolidate the request of
school heads, for preparation of Program of Work and budget. And if it falls under
the allowable expenses chargeable against the SEF the same may be charged to
the latter, procurement of which should be through the proper mode of procurement
under the RA No. 9184;

b. require the DepED and barangay officials to issue Official Receipt/Accountable
Forms 51 for every financial assistance provided by the municipality in support to the
disbursement of funds;

c. direct the Municipal Officials to request the recipients to submit the liquidation
reports, Certification from DepED Accountant that the named School Heads did
not claimed/reimbursed the registration fee and travelling expenses for the said
training and other documents to validate the proper utilization of the funds to
ensure that all disbursements are properly and completely documented, otherwise
these will be suspended/disallowed in audit;

d. instruct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Budget Officer to strictly
observe/follow updated description of accounts as prescribed under COA Circular
No. 2015-009 dated December 01, 2015;

e. require the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary reclassification and
adjustments in the General Fund book for the transactions pertaining to the
misclassified accounts in order to fairly present the financial statements; and

f. strictly adhere to the provisions of Section 4(2) of PD No. 1445 and Section 305(b)
of RA No. 7160, and develop a system of monitoring on the use of the financial
assistance to ensure that the funds are spent for the purpose for which it was
intended.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant in
coordination with the Municipal Human Resource and Management (HRM) Office to:

a. regularly reconcile the balances in accounting records with the records of the
GSIS in order to ascertain the reliability of the amount Due to GSIS in the financial
statements; and

b. effect timely and accurate remittance of GSIS premiums, loans and contributions.

We recommended and the management agreed that the Municipal Mayor and other
officials concerned to refrain from resorting to procurement and disbursement
through reimbursement basis. All payments should be made directly to the
supplier/contractor by check in order for the municipality to deduct and withhold
applicable taxes and remit the same in accordance with the BIR regulations.

We recommended that Municipal Mayor and the concerned Municipal Officials to
submit the following relevant documents in order to facilitate thorough evaluation of
the propriety of the disbursement of funds in the implementation of this project:

a. Lacking documents necessary in the progress billing payments;

b. Unsubmitted disbursement vouchers including the supporting documents
amounting to P9,476,946.79;

c. Amended contract for the change in the contracting party of the Municipality and
all other related communications in relation thereto; and

d. Notice of Suspension and/or Request for time extension to justify the extended
construction time of the project

We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:

a. Municipal Accountant to ensure the completeness of the supporting documents
as required under Section 4 (6) of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 2012-001
before processing the payment; and

b. Municipal Engineer in coordination with the Municipal Planning and Development
Officer (MPDO) to strictly and closely monitor the Construction of the New
Municipal Hall Building and ensure that execution of the project is in accordance
with the timeliness set in the contract.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor directs the:

a. Bids and Awards Committee (BAC), Municipal Planning and Development Officer
(MPDO), Municipal Engineer, Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant to
ensure the conduct of pre-procurement conference before the Procuring Entity
start with the procurement process;

b. BAC and BAC Technical Working Group (TWG) to:
- provide an explanation for the inconsistencies in the different phases of the
procurement process;
- ensure at all times that winning bidders post performance securities within the
prescribed period to make certain that the interests of the government is
adequately covered in the event it is established that a winning bidder fails to
perform any of its obligations under the contract;
- require the winning bidders to submit performance securities with a validity
period up to the final acceptance or after the defects liability period, and if
there are contract time extensions, require the contractor to submit the
corresponding extension of the security’s validity period; and
- be more cautious in evaluating the legal, technical and financial documents
submitted by bidders during bid evaluation and post-qualification.

c. BAC Secretariat to ensure the posting of warranty securities in the payment of
infrastructure projects by the contractors and demand for liquidated damages from
erring contractors for delayed completion of work and to submit all the lacking
documents immediately to the Audit Team to avoid suspension or disallowance in
audit;

d. Municipal Accountant to:
- submit a written justification on disbursing government funds for infrastructure
projects despite insufficient supporting documents;
- submit immediately all the lacking documents presented in Annex L.3 to the
Audit Team to avoid suspension/disallowance in audit; and
- ensure the completeness of the supporting documents as required under
Section 4 (6) of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 2012-001 before
processing the payment.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Bids and Awards Committee
(BAC) and Technical Working Group (TWG) to:

a. scrutinize the sufficiency of the winning bidder’s manpower and equipment on all
the projects to be awarded and not on an individual project only in cases where the
latter is the lowest bidder in several projects which have similar or overlapping
implementation period. This is to oblige the winning contractor to supplement its
manpower and/or equipment to be utilized in the projects awarded to it or to
disqualify it from obtaining several awards or contracts in conformity with the
provisions of Sections 34.1 and 34.3 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184;

b. strictly evaluate during bid evaluation and post qualification the List of All
Ongoing Government and Private Contracts and whether or not the submitted Net
Financial Contracting Capacity (NFCC) of the bidders who were awarded several
projects accurately reflect its financial capacity to carry out the work sought to be
undertaken; and

c. monitor contractors with on-going projects with other government agencies and
to coordinate with those involved in implementing infrastructure projects not
disclosed in the Statement of On-going and Completed projects to avoid
overlapping and delay in project accomplishment and/or substandard quality of
work.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Engineer and
Municipal Planning and Development Officer to strictly and closely monitor all
projects simultaneously implemented by the contractors and prepare an individual
status report per project to be submitted to the Honorable Municipal Mayor for
monitoring purposes and in order to check, verify and evaluate the quality of work
and efficiency of the said contractors.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor enjoin the Bids and Awards Committee
(BAC) to recommend appropriate method of procurement and encourage competitive
bidding instead of alternative modes, when the circumstances allow, and avoid
splitting of purchases to promote effective competition and transparency in the
procurement process and to obtain a fair and equitable contract for the best interest
of the government.

We also recommended that the management ensure that all the supporting
documents for all disbursements made are duly and properly filled-up.

We recommended and the management agreed to strictly observe the requirements
of Treasury Circular No. 02-2009 on the bonding of public officers and ensure that the
Local Chief Executive of the Municipality is covered by fidelity bond.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the MDRRM Officer to:

a. ensure that the allocation for disaster, mitigation, prevention and preparedness as
resented in the LDRRMFIP contains the details of the projects and activities to be
funded, including those planned for the utilization of the previous year’s LDRRMF
as required under Section 6.2 of NDRRMC, DBM and DILG Joint Circular No. 2013-
1 and Items 5.1.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-002;

b. meticulously plan the specific programs and projects necessary in the prevention,
mitigation and preparedness of the Municipality in case of calamity and ensure the
immediate implementation; and

c. carefully scrutinize the expenses that should be charged to the fund to avoid
disallowance in audit.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the GAD Focal Point Person to:

a) establish a GAD Database and Sex-disaggregated data as required under Section
4(b) of JMC No. 2013-01 of the DILG, DBM, NEDA and the PCW in order to create a
well-defined programs and projects that are best suited to the need of its
constituents;

b) direct the GAD Focal Point Person to properly monitor and report regularly the
status of implementation of the activities stated in the Gender and Development
Plan in order to address the gender issues; and

c) utilize the fund solely for GAD related activities or to specific programs that are
gender-responsive and are aligned to the GAD mandate.

We recommended that Management take the following courses of actions:
Direct the Accountant to immediately submit the unsubmitted CY 2018 Monthly Trial Balances and Quarterly FS and take appropriate immediate remedial measures such as augmenting the Municipal Accountant’s Office of personnel from other Departments, especially during report preparations and/or allowing them to render overtime, subject to the availability of funds, in order to comply with the timely preparation and submission of these mandatory financial reports, pursuant to mandated deadlines.  Henceforth, to prepare and submit the same, with complete Schedules and Annexes within the prescribed timeframe in accordance with the above-mentioned existing rules and regulations; and
Allow the Accountant to recommend qualified personnel of the Accounting Office to attend trainings or seminars relative to their works, to equip them with the required knowledge and skills in order to improve their performance.

We recommended that Management take the following courses of action:
Observe prompt submission of DVs and ORs including the corresponding reports each month on or before the 10th day of the ensuing month; and
Strict adherence to the cited rules and regulations on the rendition of accounts and reports be enforced by the concerned officials in order not to incur penalty as provided in Section 122 (2) of P.D. No. 1445.

We recommended that Management take the following courses of action:
Instruct the inventory committee to furnish the Auditor a copy of the inventory report of the physical count; and
Also, direct the Municipal Accountant and designated property personnel to maintain complete and updated PPE subsidiary records and conduct regular reconciliation thereof.

We recommended that Management take the following courses of action:
Prepare and submit the Report on Sources and Utilization of LDRRMF as prescribed in COA Circular No. 2012-002 to facilitate the timely review of the sources of fund, ascertain the validity of expenditures and to determine whether the programs, projects and activities were included in the LDRRMFP; and
Maintain separate RAAO for QRF, MOOE and CO that will serve as monitoring tool in determining the accuracy of balances and the amount that will be transferred to Special Trust Fund; and submit the same to the Audit Team for review and evaluation.
We recommended that Management formulate an action plan to implement the audit recommendations, and submit immediately the duly accomplished AAPSI form to the Audit Team.

We recommended that Management take the following courses of action:
Submit the GAD Plan and Budget as well as the GAD Accomplishment report to the Audit Team for evaluation; and
Management should strictly comply with the provisions of the JMC No. 2013-01 of the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA to ensure that proper planning and budgeting is provided for the GAD program and activities which will address the gender related issues and concerns identified in the GAD plan.

We reiterated the Audit Team’s previous recommendation that Management:
Require the BAC to promptly submit requests for contract review and technical inspection for completed projects to the COA to avoid unnecessary delays in the conduct of contract evaluation and technical inspection, and observe the necessity of compliance on the required documentary requirements for submission within the timeframe for review and inspection; and
Submit valid justification for our review and evaluation why the related payments to the said infrastructure projects should not be suspended in audit for failure to comply with the required submission.

We recommended that Management take the following courses of actions:
Execute lease contracts for the market space and stall holders and impose the conditions set forth in the said contracts; and
Observe the posting of the Notice Time for Collection of Tax and List of Delinquent Taxpayers to each of the 15 Barangays of the Municipality as required by R.A. No. 7160.

We recommended that Management direct the City Accountant that periodic reconciliation of the Cash in Bank Account be performed pursuant to Section 3.2 of COA Circular No. 96-011 dated October 2, 1996 to establish the accuracy of the balance of said accounts. Likewise, effect the necessary adjustments on the unrecorded cash deposit and interest income.
Moreover, prepare a letter requesting the Manager, LBP Osmeña Branch and DBP, Talisay Branch that the City Government be provided copies of its monthly bank statements thru email/online so these can be viewed/downloaded on time.

Management’s Comment:
The City Treasurer has already submitted requests to the Sangguniang Panlungsod for the passing of a resolution condoning these delinquent accounts since these stallholders were not really able to occupy the said stalls in the previous Lagtang Public Market.
We recommended that Management:
Require the different market collectors to prepare, on a periodic basis, the Report on Unpaid Stall Rentals and submit to the City Treasurer/Accountant for monitoring/recording  purposes; and
Require the City Accountant that the total realizable income from market stall rental fees is properly taken up in the books of the City Government.

Management’s Comment:
Management promised to request for the write-off of the said accounts.
We recommended that Management direct the City Accountant to:
Analyze all the receivables from NGOs/POs that are dormant, long outstanding and unsubstantiated for proper disposition, otherwise request for write-off;
Retrieve MOAs to determine the conditions of the grants;
Maintain Subsidiary Ledgers for receivables to monitor collections of loan amortizations from concerned NGOs/POs; and
Make representations with the Focal Persons to facilitate the liquidations thereof.

We recommended and  Management agreed to:
Direct the City Accountant to adopt the perpetual inventory method for supplies and materials; and
Assign staff to establish control procedures to regulate the issuance of supplies to actual requirements of end-users within a month.

Management’s Comment:
The City Health Officer promised to attached the required certification on all its requisitions.
We recommended that Management require the requisitioning officer on the procurement of drugs and medicines to attach a certification that the drug products being requisitioned or procured fall within and conform with PNDF Volume I, current edition in compliance with Paragraph II.2 of DOH AO. No. 163 s. 2002.

Management’s Comment:
The Accounting Section will make a reconciliation regarding the noted discrepancies and will inform the Audit Team of the results thereof.
We recommended that Management direct the Accounting Section to reconcile the accounts, explain the incurrence of negative balances and remit whatever balance due to the GSIS to avoid incurrence of penalties and surcharges and to enable the member beneficiaries of the City to avail of benefits from the GSIS, thus fairly present the account balance in the financial statements of the agency as of a given period. Further, we recommended that Management shall see to it that it has fully remitted monthly the employees’ and government shares.

We recommended and Management agreed to remit regularly all taxes withheld to the BIR within the prescribed time to avoid payment of penalties, surcharges and interest arising from violations and submit justifications relative thereto and toimplement the remittance of taxes withheld on time in compliance with BIR Revenue Regulation No. 2-98.

We recommended and Management agreed to direct the City Accountant to remit all PAG-IBIG and PhilHealth contributions especially those pertaining to prior years to avoid payment of interest/penalties, preclude delay in the processing of members’/employees’ claims and present the account correctly in the financial statements. We also recommended that Management direct the City Accountant to explain why the amount remitted differed from the amount withheld and to reconcile each employee’s records and ledger to determine the completeness of the contributions withheld from them.

Management’s Comment:
Management promised to implement and use the e-NGAS accounting software provided by COA to address the issue on the maintenance of the subsidiary ledgers.
We recommended that Management direct the City Accountant to prepare and submit the subsidiary schedules supporting the general ledger balances and to maintain subsidiary ledgers and records of accounts duly supported with complete documents to substantiate balances in the financial statements.

Management’s Comment:
The contract contained stipulations on the general and special conditions.
Auditor’s Rejoinder:
Not all of the terms and conditions required under Philippine Bidding Documents were included in the contract. Furthermore, the duration of the contract and the schedule of implementation were not also provided in the contract. Hence, the determination of whether the project implementation is still on time cannot be reasonably determined.
We recommended that Management:
Direct the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to explain in writing why the Bidding Documents did not include the General and Special Conditions of the Contract;
Require the supplier to submit the implementation schedule and the schedule of requirements and;
Direct the BAC to include in the Bidding Documents the General and Special Conditions of the Contract in the future public biddings, as part of the contract.

Management’s Comment:
The City did not yet execute contracts with the stallholders because there is a possibility that the stallholders might want to introduce improvements to the rented stall which could not be possibly made because the project was not yet turned over by the contractor.
Auditor’s Rejoinder:
The City can still execute the contract with the stallholders despite the pendency of the turnover of the project. The City can stipulate that the rented stall cannot be modified as of the time being.
We recommended that the City Government of Talisay execute a yearly contract of lease embodied in a public instrument for every public market stall rental and follow the standards/guidelines provided under COA Circular No. 88-282A to ensure that interests of both parties are protected, ambiguities and/or misunderstandings can be avoided.

Management’s Comment:
Management promised to comply with the recommendations and to submit the PhilGEPS registration numbers of the hired consultants.
We recommended that Management:
Require the BAC to include in the Terms of Reference the expected output/deliverables of the consultancy contract in order to properly quantify the progress and the extent of the services rendered by the consultants; and
Require the hired consultants to submit their PhilGEPS registration number.

Management’s Comment:
There was no splitting of contracts in the above transaction.
Auditor’s Rejoinder:
The Auditor did not agree with Management that the procurement did not result to splitting of contracts. The indicia discussed were very apparent that there was indeed splitting of contracts.
We recommended that Management direct the BAC to give an explanation and justification of the splitting of the contracts.

Management’s Comment:
Management promised to require the contractors to refund the disallowed amount for the road concreting projects. As regards to the project in Tabunok Public Market, the Management will install the cited lacking items and will request for a re-inspection of the project.
We recommended that Management:
Direct the City Engineer to submit a justification and explanation why the contracts were reported as 100% complete despite the noted variances in the road concreting as compared with the program of work;
Remind and require the City Engineer to be more circumspect in the inspection of actual accomplishment of projects considering that it is his mandated duty to administer, coordinate, supervise, and control the construction, maintenance, improvement, and repair of roads, bridges, and other engineering and public works projects of the local government unit as provided in Section 477 of the Local Government Code; and
Require the contractors to settle the deficiencies in the amount of P600,521.61 as it constitutes irregular expenditures.

Management’s Comment:
The Schools Division Superintendent promised to submit the SIP and DEDP.
We recommended that Management:
Require the Local School Board (LSB) to conduct the proper planning of the procurement activities in order to carry out the priority projects and activities chargeable to the SEF; and
Require the DepEd – Talisay City Division to submit the SIP and DEDP for the year 2018 in order to determine whether or not the expenditures charged against the SEF are net of the budgetary provision for the same or related item(s) in the budget for the DepEd and the ECCD Council.

We recommended and Management agreed that the LSB formulate a three-year program for the strategic allocation of the SEF. Likewise, submit the Local Board Resolutions for CY 2018, School Improvement Plans and Division Education Development Plan for auditorial review.

We recommended and Management agreed that the LSB Council incorporate in its budget programs and activities intended for ECCD pursuant to Republic Act No. 10410 and DEPED-DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 1 s, 2017.

We recommended and Management agreed that City Solid Waste Management Plan be prepared as required under Section 16 of RA 9003 for purposes of ensuring its sustainability, viability, effectiveness and relevance in relation to local and international developments in the field of solid waste management. Also, immediate creation of Barangay SWM Committee in all remaining barangays is recommended.
Moreover, to ensure efficiency and effectiveness of solid waste management program, the following were recommended:
Separation of collection for biodegradable and non-biodegradable waste pursuant to Section 21 of RA 9003.
Conversion of the City’s controlled dumpsite into an Eco-Park with Residual Containment Area (RCA) or sanitary landfill facility pursuant to Section 37 of the same RA.

We recommended and Management agreed that the city’s GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) prepare the GAD accomplishment report in accordance with Joint Circular No. 2013-001 so that proper monitoring and performance evaluation of GAD related projects/programs and activities can be made.

We recommended and  Management agreed to:
a. Direct the LDRRMC to properly identify the PAPs to support the disaster risk reduction and management activities, during the pre-disaster preparedness program and post-disaster activities; and
b. Identify the PAPs for the unexpended balance of the LDRRMF together with the current year’s allocation to ensure its efficient utilization.

We recommended and Management agreed to create and maintain an official website to enhance transparency and enforce accountability pursuant to Sections 98 and 99 of the General Provisions of the General Appropriations Act (GAA) of 2018.

We recommended that:

1.	The City Mayor direct the Inventory Committee to complete the conduct of the physical inventory of all offices including land and buildings owned by the City, prepare and submit the report on or before January 31 of each year pursuant to Section 124 of MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I, to give the Audit Team ample time to validate the report.

2	The GSO and the City Accountant to undertake serious, efficient and effective reconciliation efforts by starting with items that are easy to be counted such as land, buildings and transportation equipment as an initial approach in eliminating discrepancies in the inventory records.

3.	The GSO and Accounting Office personnel work together to reconcile the physical inventory report and accounting records and to verify all the unaccounted properties.

observations.recommendation:
Recommendation:
We recommended that:
1. The City Mayor direct the Inventory Committee to complete the
conduct of the physical inventory of all offices including land and
buildings owned by the City, prepare and submit the report on or
before January 31 of each year pursuant to Section 124 of MNGAS
for LGUs, Volume I, to give the Audit Team ample time to validate
the report.
2 The GSO and the City Accountant to undertake serious, efficient and
effective reconciliation efforts by starting with items that are easy to
be counted such as land, buildings and transportation equipment as
an initial approach in eliminating discrepancies in the inventory
records.
3. The GSO and Accounting Office personnel work together to reconcile
the physical inventory report and accounting records and to verify all
the unaccounted properties.

Recommendation:
We recommended that the incoming City Officials adhere to the terms and
conditions of any contract executed by the former City Mayor in behalf of the City
Government during his tenure of office so that the benefits that could have been
derived from the agreement/contract will be realized.
In addition, the Sangguniang Panlungsod to ascertain that all obligations of
the City covered by a contract must be provided with sufficient yearly
appropriation to enable the City to pay its obligation on time.

Recommendation:
We recommended that the City Treasurer immediately transfer the bank
balance of P2,246,668.67 from PNB to any authorized government depository banks
pursuant to DOF Circular No. 01-2017 dated May 11, 2017 and to submit
transaction documents evidencing transfer of funds to the Audit Team.

Recommendation:
We recommended that:
1. The City Mayor direct the City Housing Office (CHO) Department
Head to locate the documents relative to the individual Contract to
Sell/Memorandum of Agreement of the 165 home
partners/beneficiaries of Bayanihan Habitat Homes. In case the City
has no available documents, coordinate with the Office of the Habitat
for Humanity Philippines and request for aforesaid records.
2. Moreover, the CHO must conduct inspection on the resettlement
project determine whether the 165 home partners are occupying the
housing units.
3. The City Mayor assign the CHO to monitor the project, ensure that
home partners comply with the Agreement and enforce the provisions
of the agreement for those in default home partners.
4. The OIC- City Accountant to recognize or record the receivables from
home partners for the cost of the lot awarded to them once all the
documents are established and send demand letters to all Bayanihan
home partners with overdue amortization.
5. Demand from NOccHFH the remittance of all lot payments directly
paid by the home partners to its office. Moreover, request for the
return of all remittances made by the City representing lot payments
withhold from the employees’ salaries.

Recommendation:
We recommended that:
1. The City School Board stop the practice of charging expenses not
authorized under Section 272 of the Local Government Code and Item
4.0 of Joint Circular No. 1, series of 2017 dated January 19, 2017 against
the SEF.
2. Moreover, the City School Board must comply and consider all the
requirements in the planning and preparation of the SEF budget, ensure
that all expenditure items to be included therein shall be among those
provided under Section 4.0, shall be net of the budgetary provision for
the same or related items in the budget of the DepEd and ECCD Council
and contained in the approved School Improvement Plan (SIP) and
Division Education Development Plan (DEDP), pursuant to Section 5.0
and 5.2.1 of Joint Circular No. 1, series of 2017.

Recommendation:
We recommended that:
1. The City School Board stop the practice of charging expenses not
authorized under Section 272 of the Local Government Code and Item
4.0 of Joint Circular No. 1, series of 2017 dated January 19, 2017 against
the SEF.
2. Moreover, the City School Board must comply and consider all the
requirements in the planning and preparation of the SEF budget, ensure
that all expenditure items to be included therein shall be among those
provided under Section 4.0, shall be net of the budgetary provision for
the same or related items in the budget of the DepEd and ECCD Council
and contained in the approved School Improvement Plan (SIP) and
Division Education Development Plan (DEDP), pursuant to Section 5.0
and 5.2.1 of Joint Circular No. 1, series of 2017.

Recommendation:
We recommended that the Solid Waste Management Board review and
update the Solid Waste Management Plan to ensure sustainability, viability,
effectiveness and relevance in relation to local and international developments in the
field of solid waste management.

Recommendation:
We recommended that the City strictly comply with the requirements of
Section 1 Rule XIII of the Implementing Rules and Regulations of Republic Act
9003 to convert open dump into controlled dump. In addition, the City must
expedite the construction of a sanitary landfill as final disposal of residual waste and
to adopt a systematic, comprehensive and ecological waste management program
and encourage waste reduction/reuse/recycling and composting as strategies to
reduce the solid wastes.

Recommendation:
We recommended that the City Government adhere strictly to the provisions
of RA 9344 and DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120 dated July 4, 2012 by
allocating 1% of its IRA for the strengthening and implementation of programs,
projects and activities of the LCPC.

Recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the Accountant-designate to
ascertain the unexpended DRRMF-MOOE balance for CY 2017 and prior years
and immediately transfer the amount including the unexpended QRF of
P32,305,631.81 to the Special Trust Fund pursuant to Section 5.1.10 of COA
Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 and Section 21 of RA 10121.

Recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor submit specific legal basis for the
grant of Economic Welfare Assistance to the City officials and employees for CY
2017. Henceforth, he must ensure that benefits or incentives granted to City
officials and employees are expressly provided by law, and other statutory authority
and the rules and regulations promulgated by competent authority pursuant to
COA Circular No. 2013-003 dated January 13, 2013.

Recommendation:
We recommended that the OIC- City Accountant transfer the savings of
P329,681.93 to the General Fund for re-appropriation so that it can be used to
finance other priority programs and projects for the benefits of its constituents.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require:
1. oblige the Municipal General Services Officer to take immediate action to
facilitate the appraisal and disposal of the identified unserviceable properties
totaling to P8,589,515.11 as required of PD 1445 to prevent exposure to
different elements and further deterioration of its value;
2. submit the Report on Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment; and
3. direct the Inventory Committee to reconcile the result of the physical count
of all PPE with the property and accounting records and submit to the
Auditor not later than January 31, of each year.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal
Accountant to strictly monitor the liquidation of cash advances and to enforce
the withholding of any amount due to all accountable officers who refuses to
liquidate their outstanding cash advances despite demand and coordinate with
the Municipal Legal Officer for the institution of appropriate sanction against
the concerned AOs.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
1. Direct the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) Chairman thru the
BAC Secretariat to submit the contract, lacking pre and post bidding
documents as well as bid documents on the Construction of
Evacuation Facility Project as mentioned above to the Office of the
Auditor and to prepare design and include in the Annual Investment
47
Plan, the construction of slope protection and excavation activity as
engineering mitigation measures to address the potential problems;
2. Instruct the Municipal Budget Officer to provide appropriation for
the construction of slope protection and excavation activity to protect
the evacuees from disaster;
3. Make representation with the Sangguniang Bayan to consider/authorize
the Supplemental Budget for the engineering mitigation measures
mentioned in the preceding recommendation;
4. Direct the Municipal Engineer to submit justification/explanation on
the reported different percentage of accomplishment on the
construction of evacuation facility:
a. Consolidated Quarterly Government Programs/Projects/Activities
as of 4th Quarter of CY 2017 with accomplishment of 70.40%
submitted to the Office of the Auditor on December 26, 2017 with
corresponding Certificate of Inspection dated June 20, 2017 and
Contractor’s Statement of Work Accomplished;
b. Consolidated Quarterly Government Programs/Projects/Activities
as of 4th Quarter of CY 2017 with accomplishment of 60.64%
submitted to the Office of the Auditor on May 18, 2018.
c. Report on Fund Utilization and Status of Program/Project
Implementation as of January 29, 2018 at 68% accomplishment.
and
d. Justification on the remarkable difference on the status of work
observed by the Audit Team during inspection on February 23,
2018 and March 27, 2018.
5. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to cause the submission of the
lacking documents enumerated in the above report. Likewise, submit
proof of ownership of the lot used in the construction of the
evacuation facility; and
6. Compel the Municipal Accountant to ensure that all DVs are
properly supported with the required documents before payment and
strictly comply to the submission of accounts on the prescribed period
as required of COA Circular No. 95-006.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the GAD Focal Point
Person to:
a) establish a GAD Database and Sex-disaggregated data as required
under Section 4(b) of JMC No. 2013-01 of the DILG, DBM, NEDA
and the PCW in order to create a well-defined programs and projects
that are best suited to the need of its constituents;
b) direct the GAD Focal Point Person to properly monitor and report
regularly the status of implementation of the activities stated in the
Gender and Development Plan in order to address the gender issues;
and
c) utilize the fund solely for GAD related activities or to specific
programs that are gender-responsive and are aligned to the GAD
mandate.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Accountant to prepare the certified Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization
of Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund and the Municipal Disaster
Risk Reduction and Management Officer to submit all the required
reports/documents to the concerned agencies within the prescribed period in
consonance with the afore-cited guidelines/issuances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Accountant to strictly enforce the submission of required accounting and
financial reports within the prescribed period.
The concerned officers to look into the root cause of the delay in the
preparation and submission of the financial reports and effect appropriate
action/s when necessary, to make possible the early submission of the same.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the assigned
department head/s or personnel to fast tract the submission of the Agency Action
Plan and Status of Implementation (AAPSI) of the CY 2016 Annual Audit
Report to the Auditor concerned to facilitate monitoring and validations of the
actions taken by the LGU on the related audit observations and
recommendations, and eventually to submit a validation report to the Regional
Office IV-A, for monitoring purposes. Compliance of the same report to the
above mentioned an oversight body is necessary.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. direct the Municipal Engineer and the General Services Officer (GSO) to
ensure that a list of all on-going government PPAs in all infrastructure and
non-infrastructure projects, respectively, and those to be implemented
during the year including relevant information thereof is submitted to the
Supervising Auditor/Audit Team Leader at the beginning of the year; and
b. enjoin all Department Heads concerned of the posting to appropriate
signboards of the programs/activities for implementation by the concerned
department as required of COA Circular No. 2013-004.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct:
1. the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) Chairman thru the BAC
Secretariat and concerned personnel of the General Service Office (GSO),
to submit copies of perfected contracts/purchase orders together with the
required supporting documents and Notice of Delivery, respectively, to
the Office of the Auditor in accordance with the provisions of COA
Circular Nos. 2009-001 and 95-006 for proper evaluation and technical
review of the contracts as well as timely inspection of deliveries; and
2. the BAC Chairman or Municipal General Services Officer, as the case
maybe to monitor submission of the above-mentioned documents to avoid
suspension in audit.

We recommended that Management take the following courses of action:

e The Municipal Treasurer submit a duly certified list showing the
name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year
and submit the same to the Municipal Accountant to serve as basis in
setting up the beginning balances of RPT and SET Receivables in the
books: of accounts. In such manner, the related receivable accounts
and deferred liability will be presented fairly in the financial
statements;

e The Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant conduct
reconciliation of records to establish a realistic balance of Receivables
from Real Property Taxes and Special Education Taxes so that
necessary adjustment(s) in the books of accounts can be effected for
fair presentation of balances in the financial statements; and

e The Municipal Assessor and Municipal Treasurer reconcile their
records to determine correct and complete information used in the
computation of real property tax.

We reiterated our recommendation that the City General Services Officer or the Property Custodian conduct yearly physical count of the City properties and submit a Report on the Physical Count of PPE (RCPPE) not later than 31 January of each year pursuant to Section 124 on the Manual of the New Government Accounting System for LGUs Volume 1.  
Moreover, the City Accountant and the GSO implement the reconciliation of their records inasmuch as this deficiency has long been reported and no sincere efforts were undertaken to resolve the issue
observations.managementAction:
Created an Inventory Committee

Conducted physical count of its fixed assets to three offices namely: Legal, Accounting and Assessor’s Offices
observations.reason:
No partial report had been submitted to COA Audit Team
Leader.

observations.recommendation:
The City Accountant require the accountable officers to strictly comply with the guidelines provided in Items 5.8 of COA Circular No. 97-002 and Section 89 of P.D. 1445 in the liquidation of their cash advances.
She must monitor the liquidation and demand settlement if no liquidation was submitted within the prescribed period;
In addition, she must send final demand notices to officials and employees whose cash advances as of December 31, 2011 remained unliquidated.
 The City Mayor direct that salaries of employees whose cash advances as of December 31, 2011 has not been liquidated to this date shall be suspended pursuant to Item 9.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-004 dated November 28, 2012.
observations.managementAction:
Liquidation of cash advances were monitored by sending demand letters to accountable officers.  
Salary deduction was implemented to those employees who have not liquidated.

Prior Years’ Cash Advances were taken up as accounts receivable
observations.reason: Prior Years  Advances to Officers and Employees were transferred to Accounts Receivable.

observations.recommendation:
a. The City Accountant or her duly authorized representative coordinate with the COA Central Office and follow up the liquidation of cash advances charged against Intelligence/
Confidential Fund.

b. She must demand liquidation of all cash advances chargeable against Confidential and Intelligence Fund within 30 days after every quarter in compliance with Item 6.2.2 of Joint Circular No. 2015-01 dated January 8, 2015.
The newly elected City Mayor demand immediate settlement/liquidation of unliquidated cash advances granted before 2015 pursuant to General Guidelines of COA Circular No. 2003-003 dated July 30, 2003.

We recommended that the City Accountant submit the cash advance voucher for Intelligence/
Confidential Fund within seven (7) days after release of check in compliance with Item 6.1.8.4 of Joint Circular No. 2015-01 dated January 8, 2015.

We recommended that the City Treasurer send a written notice to the concerned payees informing them of the existence of the stale checks and advise them to request for replacement as soon as possible.

The City Accountant immediately prepare necessary adjustments in the books for stale checks in the possession of the City Treasurer so the cash in bank account is accordingly adjusted in order to reflect a fair presentation of the cash in bank balance

We recommended that management should demand payments of the loans granted to cooperatives and closely monitor compliance of their commitments, otherwise, institute legal action to collect the same, if warranted.
observations.managementAction: Demand letters were sent to farmers’ cooperative
observations.reason: Some Farmers’ Cooperatives were paying their loans already.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Accountant-Designate discontinue the practice of recording as expense the supplies and materials based on approved POs only and correspondingly recognize liability for such purchases.

It is also recommended that the City Accountant-Designate adhere strictly to the existing policy under Section 4.s of the NGAS Manual for LGUs that liability shall be recognized at the time the goods and services are accepted or rendered and supplier/creditor bills are received.

We recommended that the City of Talisay and the Local School Board refrain from charging non-related expenses to the SEF and strictly adhere to the regulations on the prioritization of expenses charged against SEF as provided under Article 363 (C) of the IRR of RA 7160 and Section 4 of DECS-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 01 s. 1998 dated April 14, 1998.
observations.managementAction: The City Officials required each schools to submit their budget at the beginning of the school year.
observations.reason:
Expenses for
utilities of
schools were
charged 
without first
exhausting 
the budget of
individual
schools.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Government strictly comply with the minimum requirements for the operation of controlled dumpsites pursuant to Section 2, Rule XVIII of Republic Act 9003 until such time that a land fill is constructed.

The City Government expedite the construction of landfill so it could comply with the requirements of R.A. 9003 in its ecological solid waste management.
observations.managementAction: The city government had purchased land for its sanitary landfill
observations.reason: Closure of open dump had been started.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Government Unit should refrain from charging salaries of   job order personnel not directly involved in environment and sanitation   to the Environment/Sanitary Services.  Fund for Environment/Sanitary Services should only be disbursed to programs/projects related to sanitation.
observations.reason:
Job Order
personnel 
were charged
to general 
services
except for 
those assigned
to sanitary and
environment
services.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor expedite the implementation of the BuB projects with funding from the NGAs as well as those solely funded by the City, so the constituents would fully enjoy the benefits that can be derived therefrom.

We also recommended that the City Mayor follow up to the concerned NGAs the immediate release of funds for other BuB projects waiting for the next tranches.
that the Municipal Treasurer to adjust the staled checks to present the correct balances of the accounts affected
observations.managementAction: Based on the report of staled checks submitted by the Municipal Treasurer.

observations.recommendation:
that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:

1. verify the liquidation of Due from Officers and Employees as of December 31, 2016
2. require officials and employees to submit supporting documents immediately or within 30 days after return to official station and refund excess
3. refrain from processing new cash advances if previous cash advance is not yet liquidated
4. issue demand letters to employees and officials who failed to liquidate cash advances within the prescribed period
observations.managementAction: The uncollected balance as of March 31, 2017 was adjusted. The Municipal Accountant issued demand letter on May 11, 2018 to the concerned officials and employees requiring that all liquidation documents have to be submitted and any remaining amount has to be settled on or before May 31, 2018. Copy of Memorandum of the Municipal Mayor dated June 19, 2018 addressed to all concerned personnel advising strict monitoring of the liquidation of cash advances was furnished to the Office of the Auditor. On June 30, 2018, salaries of Accountable Officers of cash advances were withheld and applied to their balances as reflected in the Schedule of Cash Advances transmitted to the Office of the Auditor.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Part II under Observation No. 4.

observations.recommendation: that the Municipal Accountant to record the correct RPT and SET Receivables based on the List of Taxpayers and the corresponding uncollected amounts and refrain from reverting the uncollected receivables at the end of the year to establish the correct RPT and Special Education Tax Receivables for the current year
observations.managementAction: The correct RPT and Special Education Tax Receivables for the current year 2017 have been recognized in the books, as per the RPT List of Taxpayers and the amount of tax due and collectible for the Municipal year certified by the Municipal Treasurer.

observations.recommendation:
that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:

a. Deduct/recoup mobilization fees from progress billings and reclassify entries appropriately
b. Issue collection letters to Granby and Gemwealth to collect mobilization fees
c. Review and analyze current and previous year transactions and take up necessary adjusting entries
d. Exercise care and prudence in recording transactions
observations.managementAction: Reclassifying entry for the various mobilization fees will be deducted from the succeeding progress billings. The amount of P119,820.00 has been deducted in a progress billing paid to the contractor in June 2017. The remaining amount of P284,520.00 will be deducted in the succeeding progress billings. The collection letter to Granby and Gemwealth has not yet been issued. The necessary reclassifying entries have been recognized in the books of accounts in CY 2017. Proper care and prudence were observed by the Municipality in the recording of the transactions. Entries in the Journal Entry Vouchers are carefully reviewed by the Municipal Accountant to ensure accuracy of entries.
observations.reason: Copy of the JEV was not yet furnished to the Auditor for verification.

observations.recommendation: that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to revert all long outstanding payables with no supporting documents and claimants to the unappropriated surplus of the General and Special Education Funds; ensure proper supporting documents for payables and seek validation
observations.managementAction: The necessary adjusting entry has been made on June 30, 2017.
observations.reason: After verification of the long outstanding payables with no supporting documents, claimants or have been claimed already, to the unappropriated surplus of the General and Special Education Funds.

observations.recommendation:
that the Municipal Accountant to:

a. Transfer the interest earned, RPT and Gravel and Sand from Trust Fund to the General Fund once correct reciprocal accounts established
b. determine details of accounts without corresponding reciprocal accounts and effect necessary adjustments
c. coordinate with the Provincial Accountant to reconcile records on receivables and payables of the Municipality
d. exercise care and prudence in recording transactions
observations.managementAction: At present, there are no available records of subsidiary ledgers (SLs) for these accounts. They are in the process of verifying the balances of the SLs. Once the correct amounts have been established with the reciprocal accounts, transfer of funds to the appropriate Fund will be made. Journal Entry Vouchers are carefully reviewed by the Municipal Accountant to ensure accuracy of entries.
observations.reason: The Management has not yet reconciled the balances on receivables and payables.

observations.managementAction: The absence of subsidiary records for the unutilized and unremitted balances prevented the management from returning the unexpended balances to establish the correct balances. They are in the process of verifying the balances; once the correct amount has been established, all the outstanding balance will be remitted to the proper agencies concerned.
observations.reason: The unexpended balance has not yet been remitted because the correct amount has not yet been established.

observations.recommendation:
that Municipal Mayor:

a. Refrain from granting retirement or other benefits to officials and employees of the Municipality unless supported with legal basis;
b. Direct the Municipal Budget Officer to refrain from the grant of incentives and benefits without legal basis; and
c. Direct the former Municipal Accountant to return the amount of P40,000 as retirement reward
observations.managementAction: As per the Municipal Budget Officer, this type of incentive is no longer included in the Annual Budget. The former Municipal Accountant received the Notice of Disallowance (ND No. 2017-001-100 16) on February 28, 2018. According to him, he is preparing a letter for COA.

observations.recommendation:
that Municipal Mayor:

a. Direct the Municipal Accountant and HRMO to submit the lacking documents as stated in Section 5.13 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 to enable the Audit Team verify the correctness of the amount claimed and ensure that succeeding payments of TLB are duly supported with the required documents to avoid suspension of the amount paid; and
b. Require the former Municipal Accountant to return the amount of P20,000 representing the disallowed PEI in CY 2013
observations.managementAction: The lacking documents have been completed. The former Municipal Accountant is currently employed as Job Order. As of March 31, 2018, the amount of P5,791.67 representing the balance has been deducted from him through monthly deduction.

observations.recommendation: that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Budget Officer to ensure compliance with RA 7160 and NBC No. 563 and ensure that payments of membership fees and annual dues to private organizations shall not be charged against government funds
observations.managementAction: Annual dues and membership fees are no longer charged against government funds.

observations.recommendation: that the Municipal Mayor require the supplier/contractor to provide a bank guarantee for the failure to deduct retention money and submit all bidding and contract documents for further evaluation
observations.managementAction: The supplier has agreed to provide a bank guarantee for the failure to deduct retention money. The Management will follow up with the supplier.
observations.reason: No management comment yet on whether the bank guarantee has been issued by the concerned supplier/contractor.

observations.recommendation: that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Local Council for the Protection of Children (LCPC) to prepare the Annual Work and Financial Plan (AWFP) to access and utilize the 1% LCPC fund and implement children related projects
observations.managementAction: The Budget included the LCPC fund in the current year’s Annual Budget for CY 2018.
observations.reason: In the accomplishment report, not all GAD activities implemented are written gender responsive.

observations.recommendation: that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Budget Office and the Municipal Planning and Development Officer to strictly comply with the guidelines in the DILG-DBM Joint MC No. 2011-1 on the appropriation and optimum utilization of the 20 percent Development Fund on projects that partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures
observations.managementAction: The Municipality assured compliance with the recommendation.

observations.recommendation: that the Municipal Mayor direct the GAD Focal Point Person to properly monitor and report regularly the status of implementation of the activities stated in the Gender and Development Plan and utilize the fund solely for GAD related activities or to specific programs that are gender-responsive
observations.managementAction: The GAD Focal Point Person will assure that all objectives of the programs, projects and activities written in the GAD Plan address gender issues. The Management commented that the programs, projects and activities are already being implemented though not all are gender responsive.
observations.reason: In the accomplishment report, not all GAD activities implemented are written gender responsive.

observations.recommendation: that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to submit all lacking supporting documents to validate transactions; the BAC to comply with IRR of RA 9184 and ensure completeness and validity of bidding documents; and allocate adequate time for the bidding process
observations.managementAction: The Management commented that some documents were not submitted and some processes were already being complied with. The BAC and TWG did not ensure completeness of supporting documents for procurement. Some disbursement vouchers and contracts lacked approval/certification. The Municipal officials indicated that contractor profiles are on file but documents were not sufficiently furnished to the Auditor.
observations.reason: The BAC-TWG did not ensure submission to the Auditor of sufficient supporting documents; some documents supporting procurement transactions were not submitted for verification.

observations.recommendation: that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Focal Person of the SWM to hasten completion and adoption of the Solid Waste Management Plan (SWMP) and implement waste management programs including reuse, recycling, composting, and safe closure and rehabilitation activities
observations.managementAction: The SWM coordinator averred that the SWMP has been approved and is now being implemented. The Safe Closure and Rehabilitation Plan has already been approved and implementation is pending. The municipality will convert the MRF to residual containment area and coordinate with EMB Calabarzon for further action.
observations.reason: The SWMP is pending full implementation and certain actions are still in the planning/implementation stages.

observations.recommendation: that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer, Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Assessor to submit the Deed of Absolute Sale and supporting documents and justify the additional payment
observations.managementAction: A copy of the Deed of Absolute Sale amounting to P40,000,000.00 has been provided to the Auditor for verification. No further justification was furnished to explain the discrepancy noted in the Deed of Sale and the disbursement voucher.
observations.reason: For submission of original copy of Deed of Absolute Sale and lacking documents. No justification was provided to explain the discrepancy in the Deed of Sale.

observations.managementAction:
Homeowners belonging to Sitio Matinagdanon in Dumlog have been relocated to the lot owned by Emilio Rafols.

Personnel from the Local Housing Office are regularly conducting pulong-pulong with informal settlers.

The Local Housing, with the assistance of the City Assessor & Eng’g Dept., are identifying the owner of the lot adjacent to the Employees Village so that they can negotiate for a right of way.

observations.recommendation:
We recommend that Management instruct the Bids and Awards Committee and
members of the Sangguniang Panlungsod to explain the non-compliance to the rules
and regulations in the conduct of bidding on lease of lands, buildings and other physical
structures of the City as required under Section 198 of COA Circular No. 92-386.

We further recommend that Management revisit the Lease Contract entered into by
the City Government of Talisay and Asian Marine Transport Corporation and if
feasible, have the Lease Agreement rescinded to:

a) enable the City to acquire possession of the Fish Port which costs much more
than the P9.6 Million that AMTC paid as advance lease;

b) enable the City to operate the Roro Port under the supervision of the Cebu Ports
Authority, if warranted;

c) hasten the completion of both projects, make these operational to generate
income and provide jobs to its constituents.

We recommend that Management, through the Local Housing Office:

a) Conduct regular monitoring of the housing projects to ensure the speedy
transfer of titles in the name of Talisay City, and then to the Homeowners
Association; and

b) Coordinate with other offices such as but not limited to the Offices of the City
Engineer, City Assessor, City Attorney and the Sangguniang Panlungsod, in
order to hasten site developments, rehabilitations and resolution of issues that
hamper the resettlement program of the City Government.

We recommend that the City Government:

a) Direct the General Services Office and the Bids and Awards Committee to
ensure streamlining in the procurement process by identifying the bottlenecks in
order to achieve effectiveness and efficiency in the delivery of the goods and
services to the requesting offices and departments within a reasonable time;

b) Use all possible means to extend equal opportunity to all private contracting
parties who are eligible and qualified to participate in the procurement process
to ensure that the government is able to choose the best possible quality of goods
and services at a reasonable cost. Consequently, the use of the agency website in
the posting and advertising of procurement opportunities is highly encouraged;

c) Direct the General Services Office to clearly indicate the necessary specifications
and other terms in the bidding documents to remove ambiguity and to enable the
participating suppliers to give an informed and competitive bids and quotations;

d) Direct the Bids and Awards Committee to strictly comply with the requirement
in Section 52.3 of the IRR of RA 9184 which mandates that in case of shopping,
at least three (3) price quotations from bona fide suppliers shall be obtained;

e) Direct the General Services Office to refrain from referring to brand names in
the specifications of goods as provided in Section 18 in the IRR of RA 9184.

We recommend that Management:

c) Request TCSC and TC for the renewal of their expired performance security
considering that the projects are still not completed; and

d) Determine the reasons for their non-completion otherwise consider terminating
their contracts, if warranted.

We recommend that the City Government:

a) Direct the Bids and Awards Committee to explain why the mode of procurement
as recommended and approved in the BAC Resolution is not the mode of
procurement posted in thePhilGEPS;

b) Direct the Bids and Awards Committee to follow strictly the policies and
procedures prescribed in the Revised IRR of RA 9184.

We recommend that the City Government:

a) Direct the Bids and Awards Committee to explain why they have not declared
the winning bidder as ineligible because it did not comply with one of the
eligibility requirements;

b) Direct the Bids and Awards Committee to follow strictly the policies and
procedures prescribed by the Revised IRR of 9184 and the Local Government
Units Procurement Manual in the screening for eligibility of bidders.

We recommend that the City Government:

a) Direct the General Services Office to prepare all purchase orders in complete
details as provided in COA Circular No. 2009-001;

b) Direct all personnel involved in the procurement process to prepare all bidding
documents in accordance with COA Circular No. 2009-001 and RA 9184 in
order to promote effectiveness and efficiency in the procurement process and to
avoid untoward circumstances that would be unfavorable to the government.

We recommend that the City Government:

a) Direct the City Accountant to rectify the bank reconciliation statements
submitted for the months of March to November 2016 in order to determine the
correct cash balances per book and per bank taking into consideration the items
that should not be anymore included in the reconciling items;

b) Remind the City Accountant to properly review the financial reports before
their submission to the intended users;

c) Require a written explanation from the City Accountant why the negotiated
checks were still included in the outstanding checks as reconciling items.

We recommend that the City Government:

a) Direct the City Accountant to adjust the Cash in Bank-Local Currency, Current
Account by reducing it by P76,979.91 which is the amount of overstatement of
the already closed current account;

b) Further, instruct the City Treasurer to deposit intact the collections for the day
and to make the deposits on the day of collection or the next following banking
day.

We recommend that Management:

a) Conduct an appraisal of all lots acquired through donations or transfers without
cost and come up with their fair value;

b) Cause the Accountant to book the fair values of the lands, when available;

c) Direct the City Legal Officer, City Assessor and other officials to facilitate and
expedite the transfer of ownership of the lots without titles to protect the interest
of the city government.

We recommend that Management direct the City Legal Officer, City Assessor,
General Services Officer and other officials to facilitate and expedite the transfer of
ownership of the lots in the name of the City to protect the interest of the
government.

We recommend that Management instruct:

a) The General Services Office to revise their Inventory Report of Land Owned by
the LGU to include relevant data such as OCT/TCT, land area, lot no,
acquisition cost, etc.

b) The Accounting Office to provide a complete description of the transaction in
preparing the JEV in order to come up with accurate subsidiary ledger for each
lot;

c) The GSO and Accounting Office to conduct periodic reconciliation of the
records of both offices in order to present the correct balance of the Land
account.

We recommend that Management:

a) Direct the General Services Office to conduct an actual physical count of all
properties of LGU-City of Talisay to verify the completeness and existence of
inventory and property in compliance with Section 124 of the New Government
Accounting System for LGUs, Volume 2;

b) Direct the City Accountant to strictly comply with PPSAS 17 especially in the
provision of depreciation in all PPE items and to effect the depreciation of those
undepreciated PPE items over their remaining useful life;

c) Require the City Accountant to correct the account balances of those two PPE
items carried at negative net book values

d) Require the General Services Office to furnish the Accounting Office of the list
of unserviceable property awaiting disposition for its transfer to Other Assets;

e) Exert efforts to locate the missing properties and make a demand to the end
users for the return of the missing items.

We recommend that the City Government:

a) Direct the City Accountant to reclassify the erroneous items in the Other
Supplies and Materials Inventory to their correct account classification, as
follows:
Correct Account Classification Balance
General Fund
Disaster Response and Rescue Equipment 239,950.00
Medical Equipment 108,800.00
Technical and Scientific Equipment 170,000.00

Small Items - Computer Peripherals* 2,860.00
Small Items - Hardware and Construction Supplies* 29,800.00

Small Items - Military and Police Supplies* 1,002,868.32

Grand Total 4,933,682.32
Correct Account Classification Balance
*small items totaling P4,414,932.32

b) Direct the City Accountant to record as expense the small value items issued and
included in the Other Supplies and Materials Inventory for a fair presentation of
the account in the financial statement.

We recommend that Management direct the City Accountant to record in the City’s
books the total amount of P29,360,762.70 under Receivables-Disallowances/Charges
account which is the total disallowance in COA Order of Execution (COE) No. 2015-
179.

We recommend that Management direct the Accounting Section to reconcile the
reciprocal accounts and effect necessary adjustments to reflect the correct account
balances and present reliable data on the agency’s operation as of a given period.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the Local School Board (LSB) to:

a. provide legal basis on the provision of snacks to grade school students during the
Local Cultural & Heritage Immersion ;

b. require the persons responsible to explain/justify in writing the incurrence of
P300,375.00 representing payment of snacks for December 18-23, 26-30, 2016
which were declared by DepED Order No. 9, s. 2015 as Christmas break while
December 19, 20, 26 & 27 falls on a weekend (Saturday/Sunday) and the excess
snacks amounting to P68,425.00;

c. explain/justify why the dates of actual delivery of snacks per Delivery Receipts
differ in the attached AS;

d. explain/justify why the activity for Local Culture and Heritage Immersion
included 644 people identified as dancers, haulers, drivers, real angels, moving
angels and others;

e. instruct Papa Love Lechon Manok to refund the excess payment of P2,250.00;
and,

f. utilize SEF funds in accordance with the prioritized programs as provided under
Section 100(c) and 272 of RA 7160 which gives utmost priority for the repair and
rehabilitation of school buildings and facilities.

We recommend that Management explain the use of the SEF for expenses not in
accordance with the purpose. Further, we also recommend that Management
immediately stop the practice of using the SEF outside the scope for which this fund
was established and observe the proper utilization thereof pursuant to the
provisions of DECS, DBM and DILG Joint Circular Nos. 1, series of 1998, 01-A
series of 2000 and 01-B series of 2001 and Section 100(c) and 272 of the Local
Government Code of 1991. Likewise, we recommend for the refund of P794,678.50
which was paid to various payees since payment thereof was contrary to the IRR of
R.A. 9184.

We recommend that the Local School Board (LSB) terminate the time deposits and
use the funds for the repair of school buildings and the provision of facilities,
equipment and textbooks among the different schools of the City as determined by
the School Board.

Henceforth, we recommend to the LSB that SEF funds be utilized in accordance
with the prioritized programs as provided under Section 100 and 272 of RA 7160
which gives utmost priority for the repair and rehabilitation of school buildings and
facilities instead of investing funds in time deposits.

We recommend that Management establish an Internal Audit Service office and
install a sound system of internal control to strengthen accountability, ensure
ethical, economical, efficient and effective operations, improve the quality and
quantity of outputs and enable agencies to better respond to the requirements of the
public they servein compliance with AO No. 70, NGICS and Section 124 of PD 1445.

We recommend that Management instruct the Local Disaster Risk Reduction
Officer to submit the Monthly Report on the Sources and Utilization of Disaster
Risk Reduction Management on or before the 15th day after the end of each month
to the Office of the Auditor pursuant to Section 5.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012-002
to facilitate the review and monitoring of disbursements.

We also recommend that Management direct the Accounting Section to disclose the
amount and details of the unexpended balance of the LDRRMF in the Notes to
Financial Statements topresent reliable data of the funds operation.

We recommend that Management ensure that all disbursement of government funds
be supported with complete documentation.

We recommend the refund of P350,000.00 which was paid to the supplier of the
engine because procurement thereof was contrary to the IRR of R.A. 9184, thus
making the disbursement irregular.

Recommendation:

We recommended that Management direct the Bids and Award Committee (BAC)

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor should require the General Services Office and the Accounting Office to prepare PCs and PPELCs compliant with the prescribed form and containing the required information to facilitate the reconciliation of the PPE accounts of both offices.

We recommended that Purchase Orders (POs) as a contract by and between the government and the supplier should properly indicate the delivery date so that the City Government of Talisay can implement damages on late deliveries.

We recommended compliance with the provision of Section of the Manual on NGAS for LGUs, Vol. I, that the City Treasurer shall prepare and submit to the Office of the City Accountant the duly certified list of taxpayers with the corresponding amount due and collectible based on Real Property Tax Account Register/Taxpayer’s Index Card and assessment roll of the City Assessor’s Offices which served as the basis in setting up the RPT and SEF Receivables at the beginning of the year.
observations.managementAction: The Office of the Treasurer had submitted the duly certified list of taxpayers with the corresponding amount due and collectible based on Real Property Tax Account Register/Taxpayer’s Index Card and assessment roll of the City Assessor’s Offices which served as the basis in setting up the RPT and SEF Receivables at the beginning of the year.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City should abide with the existing rules and regulation on the utilization of the 20% Development Fund and submit justification why payments were charged to said fund instead of the General Fund.

We recommended that the City Mayor should instruct the Accountant designate to transfer the unexpended/unobligated of the balance of the Quick Response Fund and DRRMF-MOOE to the Special Trust Fund and also a Report on the Receipt and utilization of the DRRMF sourced from GAA shall be furnished the COA Auditor on the 5th day following the end of each month.
observations.managementAction: The City Accountant had transferred part of the Quick Response Fund and DRRMF to the Special Trust Fund.
observations.reason: The balance of the fund for CY 2013 had not been transferred since it entails a big amount which the GF could not sustain. A Report on the Receipt and Utilization of the DRRMF was not submitted to the Office of the Audit Team Leader on the 5th day of the end of each month.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LGU implement the minimum employees’ net take home pay of P3,000.00 monthly. The City Accountant designate should direct payroll clerks to observe the limitations set and the order of preference imposed in authorized deductions as prescribed in the GAA of 2015.
observations.managementAction: Management did not allow employees to renew or make new loans to other sources if their net monthly take home pay is less than P3,000.00.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the management return immediately the remaining PDAF funds to the Bureau of Treasury pursuant to SC decision.
observations.managementAction: Management has already deposited to the Bureau of Treasury the remaining balance of P3,072,004.00 on June 2016.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the management comply with DBM-NEDA-NCRFW Joint Circular No. 2014-1 in the utilization of the GAD Fund.
observations.managementAction: The city government promised that insurance for barangay official will not be charged to GAD Fund .
